docs.text:
Useful, but not the point of this repo — the background I lean on while building. Starting from scratch? Data Structures, then Algorithms. Here for the deep end? Networks, read in order. Brushing up for interviews? The Machine Learning notes end in collapsible Q&A cards.

  • Networks

    Random graphs, concentration, the giant component, branching processes, small worlds, PageRank and epidemics. The most developed section — proof-heavy, best read in order. 18 notes.

    Start with graph counting tricks

  • Machine Learning

    Probability and MLE/MAP through regression, classifiers, optimisation, neural nets, CNNs, transformers and language models. Every note ends in Q&A cards. 11 notes.

    Start with probability

  • Data Structures

    Arrays, lists, trees, hash maps, heaps and graphs — intuition, invariants, complexity. The classic first course, in my own words. 18 notes.

    Start with: what is a data structure?

  • Algorithms

    Divide and conquer, graph traversal, shortest paths, spanning trees, linear programming and reductions — the patterns behind LeetCode-style problems. 8 notes.

    Start with divide and conquer

    Reading notes on hello-algo — re-typing the book's implementations from memory as active recall, chapter by chapter. 1 note.

docs.title: Blog — Paper Notes
docs.text:
More learning log than research blog. Short writeups on papers I'm reading, each aiming for:

  • the key idea in plain language
  • the key result, and why it matters
  • one naive question or extension of my own

Right now I care most about learning to recognise a genuinely valuable idea — and staying ahead of where AI is going. Two threads:

  • Agent safety — I'm about to start as an agent-safety intern, so I'm reading into that area first.
  • Notable papers from the top ML and AI conferences.

I'm not pretending to be an expert; this is a record of my own learning process.

docs.title: Divide and Conquer
docs.text: Children：MergeSort

docs.title: Prove Correctness
docs.text:
1. Base case: n = 1 is correct
  2. Inductive hypothesis: assume n/2 is correct
  3. Inductive step: combine step is correct ​思考​的​时候​也​是​一样​，​比如​我​把​一个​问题​拆成​两​部分​分给​下面​人​做​，​然后​下面​人​也​分成​两​部分​去​做​，​那么​base case，​最​底下​的​实习生​一定​是​只​handle​一个​base case​不会​出错​，​他​的​上​一级​只​需要​拿到​两个​实习生​的​东西​合并​compare，​只要​这步​不错​，​到​我​这里​一定​不会错​

​做题​的​时候​也​这样​想​，​不想​recursion，​而​想​的​是​ - ​切​ (Divide): ​假如​我​把​数据​从​中间​切​一刀​，​分成​上半部​和​下​半部​（​或​左​半边​和​右​半边​）。

  • ​甩锅​ (Recurse): ​假如​我​已经​知道​了​上半部​的​答案​，​也​知道​了​下​半部​的​答案​。

  • ​缝合​ (Combine): ​我​利用​某种​性质​，​把​它们​拼​起来​，​或者​利用​其中​一个​答案​去​限制​另​一个​的​搜索​范围​。

Ex： - Pareto Optimality - Maximum Subarray Sum - Monotone Matrices: ​先​找到​第​n/2​行​干活​，​然后​派​给​下属​

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
two recursion + one for loop 

def solve(arr):
    n = len(arr)

    // Base Case
    if n == 1: return arr

    // Step 1: Divide (​切分​)
    mid = n // 2
    left_part = arr[:mid]
    right_part = arr[mid:]

    // Step 2: Two Recursions (​负责​ N log N ​里​的​ "2T(N/2)")
    // ​这​一步​是​把​锅​甩​给​下属​
    L_result = solve(left_part) 
    R_result = solve(right_part)

    //Step 3: One For Loop (​负责​ N log N ​里​的​ "O(N)")
    // ​这​一步​是​ Combine，​是​你​自己​要​干​的​活​（​线性​扫描​）
    // ​比如​在​ Pareto ​题里​：for p in L_result: if p.y < max_y ...
    // ​比如​在​ Merge Sort ​里​：while i < len(L) and j < len(R) ...
    final_result = combine(L_result, R_result) 

    return final_result

docs.title: BFS (Breadth-First Search)
docs.text:
• ​按​距离​访问​
  • Use queue(FIFO), O(|V| + |E|) algorithms: 1. Add G.root to the queue 2. While queue not empty: pop node from the first in the queue and visit; add neighbors to the queue if not visited
  • Single-source shortest paths

docs.title: DFS (Depth-First Search)
docs.text:
• Explore a neighbor's entire subgraph before moving to next neighbor
  • Use stack(LIFO), O(|V| + |E|)
  • Both pre-order, in-order, post-order are DFS
  • algorithm:
      1. preorder.add(start)
      1. visited.add(start)
      1. for each neighbor of start:
      2. if neighbor not visited:
        • DFS(neightbor)
      1. postorder.add(start)

docs.title: Linear programming
docs.text: Related: Optimization · Flows in networks

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
1. It can be either a maximization or a minimization problem
  2. Its constraints can be equations and/or inequalities
  3. Variables are often restricted by non-negativity, tho not necessary
  4. Feasible region: vertices will be optimal
  5. Canonical form (means the most standard and simple express, we try to transform into this form when solving LP problems): $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{maximize} && c^T x \ & \text{subject to (s.t.)} && Ax \le b \ & && x \ge 0 \end{aligned} $$

docs.title: Duality
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} & \text{maximize}  && 3x_1 +6x_2-7x_3 \\ & \text{subject to (s.t.)} && x_1+x_2+x_3 \le 100 \\ &                    && 6x_2-7x_3 \le 20 \\ &                    && x_1+x_2 \le 32 \\ &                    && x_1,x_2,x_3 \ge 0 \end{aligned} \]

docs.text: Related: Greedy Algorithm

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• Spanning trees
    • contains all vertexs
    • tree:
      • E = (V - 1)
      • Acyclic
      • Connected
  • Minimum spanning tree: spanning tree with minimum weight

docs.title: The Cut Property
docs.text:
• Cut: divide all vertices into two groups without overlap
  • Crossing edge: edge that connects two groups
  • Minimum-weight crossing edge must be in MST
  • Prove: Suppose MST does not include cheapest crossing edge. It uses another crossing edge. Then if we remove e_heavy with e_min, the total weight has decreased

docs.title: Prim's Algorithm
docs.text:
• Start with a vertex, add that to MST
  • S: explored vertices in MST
  • V-S: to be explored
  • Find the cheapest crossing edge between those two groups of vertices.

docs.title: Kruskal's Algorithm
docs.text:
• look at the entire map at once. Don't care about a "headquarters"
  • easier with sparse graphs
  • relies on Union-Find (Disjoint Set) to check if adding an edge will create a cycle
  • Algorithm:
      1. order the edges in increasing order by weight using PQ
      1. While there are still vertices left: 
      2. Find the minimum weight edge;
        • If adding it does not create a cycle: Add the edge to the MST

**

docs.title: Dijkstra (works for nonnegative edges)
docs.text: Goal： output the shortest path for every source Algorithm: 1. create a priority queue 2. add source vertex s to PQ with priority 0, add all other sources vertex as unlimited 3. until the PQ is empty, remove the vertex with the lowest priority from the PQ and relax all of its outgoing edges (update priority of neighboring vertices). when updating, it follows BFS (uses PQ)

docs.title: A* （heuristic）
docs.text: ​有​方向感​、​有​目标​ - heuristic value is given \(f(n) = g(n) + h(n)\) - f(n)​是​潜力​值​，g(n) is actual path value you took, h(n) is future heuristic value - h（F）= 0 means it is the final destination - h(A) = 9 means at A, you ETA is 9

docs.title: Core definition
docs.text:
• performance advantages:
    • space-efficient representation for a sequence of variables
    • random access to any given variable in the sequence is a fast, constant-time operation.
  • disadvantage: size-changing is slow
    • array-backed lists is constant

docs.title: Asymptotic
docs.text:
General rule: O(1) \(\le\) O(N) \(\le\) O(N logN) \(\le O(N^a)\) \(\le O(b^N)\) \(\le\) O(N!)

  • Always drop constant and lower-order terms
  • Θ：bounded on both side, equal
  • O: Upper bound
  • Ω: lower bound
  • \[\text{Total Runtime} \approx (\text{Max Work per Level}) \times (\text{Number of Levels})\]

docs.title: loop
docs.text:

    for (int i = 1; i < N; i += 1)

  • ​第三项​影响​

​代码​结构​ ​每​一层​的​总​工作量​ ​复杂度​ ​例子​ ​单路​递归​ q(N/2) ​衰减​ (\(N, N/2, N/4...\)) \(O(N)\) \(\text{Total} = N + \frac{N}{2} + \frac{N}{4} + \frac{N}{8} + \dots + 1\) ​双路​递归​ 2 * q(N/2) ​持平​ (\(N, N, N...\)) \(O(N \log N)\) Merge Sort​调用​两次​递归​mergeSort（N/2） ​无​苦力​ q(N/2) ​仅​递归​ (\(1, 1, 1...\)) \(O(\log N)\) Binary Search

docs.title: Heap sort(​堆​)
docs.text:
Naive Algorithm:
Input: an array A[0,..., N-1]
1. Create a new empty max-heap array H
2. for all x in A:
    H.insert(x)
3. Create a new array T that has size N
4. for i from N-1 to 0:
    max = H.deleteMax()
    B[i] = max
5. return B

HeapSort - Θ(NlogN)

docs.title: 1.1 ADT: abstract data types that are defined only by its operations.
docs.text:
what + rules, expected Big-O - List: get/add/remove ​按照​index, ordered, duplicate allowed - Set: add/contains/remove, no duplicates - Map: put/get/containsKey ​用​key​找​value - Queue: FIFO - Stack: push/pop/top LIFO

Interface：​表达​ADT​的​一种​语言​工具​, defines what operation exists

docs.text:
new xxx()

  1. ​底层​结构​
  2. ​性能​特征​
    • HashMap：​平均​ O(1) ​查找​，​不​排序​

docs.title: Hashing
docs.text: the process of converting complex data into a single interger

docs.title: Core definition
docs.text:
• valid
    • consistency: x.hashCode() will always return the same thing
    • deterministic: if x.equals(y) then x.hashCode() must equal y.hashCode()
    • but two objects can have the same hash code and be unequal
    • if two objects have different hash codes, they must be unequal.
  • good
    • minimal collisions: distribute elements evenly
    • speed

docs.title: Build the actual table
docs.text:
• External chaining: handle collisions
    • Bucket 2: [ "Cat" ] \(\rightarrow\) [ "Dog" ] \(\rightarrow\) null
  • Load Factor: # elements / # buckets; if it hits a certain limit, the table is too crowded and will resize
  • Resizing process:
      1. create larger array: double
      1. Re-has everything
    • takes O(N)

docs.text:

docs.title: Heap
docs.text: Related: HeapSort

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
1. Shape property: the tree is complete (filled level by level, left to right)
  2. Heap property:
    1. max-heap: every node \(\ge\) its children. Root is max
    2. min-heap
    3. ​没有​左小右​大​这个​要求​
  3. Array representation following these properties:
    1. Empty 0th index
    2. leftChild(k) = 2k
    3. rightChild(k) = 2k + 1
    4. parent(k) = k/2
  4. Sink:
    1. min-heap: ​找同​一层​里​最小​的​更换​
    2. max-heap: ​找​更​大​的​更换​

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
1. Insertion
    • Insert 0 into the min-heap [-, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 6]
    • Put 0 at the end of the heap[-, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 6, 0]
    • While inserted node with parent:
      • If inserted node is smaller, swap
        • [-, 1, 2, 3, 0, 5, 7, 8, 6, 4]
        • [-, 1, 0, 3, 2, 5, 7, 8, 6, 4]
        • [-, 0, 1, 3, 2, 5, 7, 8, 6, 4]
      • If not, stop
    • Stop
  2. Deletion:
    • Remove the root node (smallest) from the min-heap [-, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 6]
    • Make the last element the new root [-, 6, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8]
    • Sink the node until its in the right place (​要​找到​当前​层里​最小​的​孩子​，​有​可能​一次​就​找到​了​，​也​有​可能​要​找遍​所有​)
      • [-, 2, 6, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8]
      • [-, 2, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7, 8]

docs.title: In-place algorithm
docs.text:
1. Each array naturally can form a heap formation according to heap representation and shape property
  2. Build-heap O(N): Find the last non-leaf node: index \(\le\) [N/2] and sink Time is not O(NlogN) because not every node need sink and each node will have limited floors to sink, so its linear
  3. Find the max and switch with A[last index], redo sink

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
• insert: O(N)
  • deleteMax: O(logN)
  • Total: O(N logN)

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Repeat for i = 0 to N - 1:
2. Designate item i as the traveling item.
3. Swap item backwards until traveller is in the right place among all previously examined items.
  • use j pointer to track current spot of traveling item. demo: cs61b in-place insertion sort demo.pdf

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text: best case: O(1) worst case: \(Θ(N^2)\)

docs.title: OOP
docs.text: Object-Oriented Programming focusing on Inheritance, Polymorphism and Casting.

docs.title: Phase 1: read code
docs.text:
1. The inheritance hierarchy inheritance works down, not up
  2. Parent: Taiko
  3. Children: Chu extends Taiko, Shime extends Taiko (Chu and Shime are siblings so not related to each other)
  4. play() behavior: Taiko: returns "BOOM" Chu: returns "don kon" Shime: returns super.play() (super is Taiko), returns "BOOM"
  5. equals() method Usually == check if objects are the exact same instance in memory (reference equality) 
    public class Taiko {
    public boolean equals(Object o) //Object is an universal class in java. So we need casting here to tell java that o is a Taiko
        return ((Taiko) o).play().equals(this.play()) 
}

docs.title: QuickSort
docs.text:
QuickSort is the fastest only if we make the right decisions about pivot selection; partition algorithm; how to deal with the worst case.

Partitioning: to rearrange the array such that all items to the left of the pivot are \(\le\) pivot. The pivot can move during the process.

docs.title: Algorithm：​按​pivot​大小​重排​，​把​小​的​放​左​，​大​的​放右
docs.text:
1. Partition on the leftmost item as the pivot
  2. Recursively quick sort the left half.
  3. Recursively quick sort the right half

docs.title: QuickSort vs MergeSort
docs.text:
• MergeSort​稳定​, merge​是从​中间​切开​，​只​跟​下标​有关​
  • QuickSort​会​取决于​pivot choice
  • Compare to Merge sort: on average, quick sort is \(Θ(NlogN)\).

docs.title: Quicksort L3S
docs.text:
• Always leftmost pivot selection
  • Partition Algorithm: 3-scan (A three-pass (three scans) partition that splits the array into < pivot, == pivot, > pivot using an extra array.)
  • Worst Case avoidance strategy: shuffle

docs.title: Quicksort LTHS (Fix partition algorithm, faster than Mergesort if pivot good)
docs.text:
• Tony Hoare Partitioning: important pivot selection strategy
  • ​选中​pivot L: A[0]，
  • create L and G pointers at left and right ends: L = pivot + 1 (hate large or equal items) G = ​最​右边​ (hate small or equal items)
  • Walk pointers towards each other, stopping on a hated item:
    • When both pointer stopped, swap
    • Move pointer by one
    • When pointers cross, done walking
  • Swap pivot with G

docs.title: QuickSort
docs.text:
• calculate the exact median: slower than MergeSort
  • Cost to compute medians is too high
  • Have to live with worst case

docs.title: QuickSelect
docs.text: Goal: find the median using partitioning

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
• Best case: \(Θ(NlogN)\)
    • ​每层​工作量​N：​第一层​partition：N
      • ​第二层​：N/2 + N/2
      • ...
    • ​一共​有​几层​取决于​N​能​除以​2​几次​，​所以​是​logN
  • Worst case: \(Θ(N^2)\)
  • Compare to Merge sort: on average, quick sort is \(Θ(NlogN)\).

docs.title: RadixSort
docs.text:
• key is a fixed d digit
  • radix：​进制​的​base
    • ​十进制​：radix = 10
    • ​英文字母​：radix = 26
    • ​字节​：256
  • a sort that works with one character at a time (by grouping objects that have the same digit in the same position).
  • Symbols:   N = number of keys   W = length of longest key (digits / chars)   R = radix (alphabet size of each digit)

docs.title: LSD

  • Time Θ(WN + WR) ≈ Θ(WN)

docs.title: MSD
docs.text: • left to right   - Worst-case Θ(WN + WR) (same as LSD)   - Best-case (only need top digit, R > N): Θ(N + R)

docs.text: Related: B-Trees

docs.title: Left Leaning Red Black Trees (LLRB)
docs.text:
a representation of B-trees that are easier to work with in code

runtime：O（logN）

docs.title: B-Trees conversion to LLRB
docs.text:
• A four node: Pick the middle value to be the black parent, and the other two become red children
  • A three node: The larger value becomes the Black Parent and the smaller value becomes the Red Left Child
  • A two node: become black child, left and right depends on value compared with parent.

docs.title: Rotation operations
docs.text:
When we do insertions, we will break LLRB rules, so we use the following three operations to fix.

  1. rotateLeft(A) - fix Red Right Child
  2. rotateRight(A) - fix consecutive red links (left child red AND left-left grandchild red)
  3. colorFlip(A) - fix 2 red children

    so rotateRight is almost always followed with colorFlip(A)

docs.title: Insertion
docs.text:
• Rule：new insertion node is always red and also merge this node temporarily into current node
  • ​如果​操作​到​最后​只​剩下​最后​root​的​两条​边​是​红​的话​，​也​要​colorFlip，​这样​都​边​黑​了​

docs.title: Sorting and Selecting
docs.text: Related: Comparison-based sort · Counting-based sort

docs.title: Core definitions
docs.text:
• an ordering relation:
    • Law of Trichotomy: One of a << b, a = b, b << a is true.
    • Law of Transitivity: If a << b, and b << c, then a << c.
    • Total order: with the properties above
  • sort: permutation of a sequence of elements that puts the keys into non-decreasing order
  • ** "the" and "get" are equal in ordering but not equal in .equals()**
  • Inversion: ​逆序​对​ given a sequence of elements with Z inversions, perform some sequence of operations to reduce the total number of inversions to zero.

docs.title: LSD vs comparison sort
docs.text:
• Comparison sort: Θ(N log N)
  • LSD: Θ(WN)   - When N huge, W small → LSD wins   - When strings very long (W large), N moderate → comparison sort can be faster

docs.title: Stability, Adaptiveness and Optimization
docs.text:
• A sort is stable if the order of equivalent elements is preserved. ​比如​同样​是​3​的​3​个​同学​，sort​前​什么​顺序​sort​后​什么​顺序​
  • A sort that is adaptive exploits the existing order of the array.
    • Examples are InsertionSort, SmoothSort, and TimSort：
      • Switch to Insertion Sort - When a subproblem reaches size 15 or lower, use insertion sort. It is very very fast for inputs of small sizes.
      • Exploit restrictions on set of keys - For example, if the number of keys is some constant, we can use this constraint to sort faster by applying 3-way QuickSort.
      • Switch from QuickSort - If the recursion goes too deep, switch to a different type of sort.

docs.title: 1. ​通俗​比喻​：​帮派​老大​ (The Boss Analogy)
docs.text:
​想象​一所​学校​里​有​很多​学生​，​大家​一​开始​都​不​认识​，​每个​人​自成​一派​。

  • ​规则​： ​每个​帮派​都​有​一个​“​老大​” (Representative)。

  • ​初始状态​： ​每个​人​都​是​自己​的​老大​。

Union-Find ​只​需要​支持​两个​操作​：

docs.title: 操作​ A: Find(x) —— “​你​老大​是​谁​？”
docs.text:
​如果​你​想​知道​学生​ A ​和​学生​ B ​是不是​一伙​的​，​你​不​需要​去查​族谱​。​你​只​需要​问​他们​：

  • A：“​你​老大​是​谁​？” \(\rightarrow\) A ​说​：“​是​ E。”

  • B：“​你​老大​是​谁​？” \(\rightarrow\) B ​说​：“​是​ E。”

  • ​结论​： ​既然​老大​一样​，​那​你们​就是​同一个​帮派​的​（​已经​连通​了​）。

docs.title: 操作​ B: Union(x, y) —— “​帮派​合并​”
docs.text:
​现在​学生​ A ​的​帮派​想​和​学生​ C ​的​帮派​合并​。

  • A ​的​老大​是​ E。

  • C ​的​老大​是​ F。

  • ​合并​方法​： ​很​简单​，​让​其中​一个​老大​给​另​一个​当​小弟​就行了​。​比如​让​ E ​认​ F ​做​老大​。

  • ​结果​： ​以后​ A ​再​被​问​“​老大​是​谁​”，​他会​找​ E，E ​会​找​ F，​最后​ A ​会​说​：“​我​现在​跟​ F ​混​了​”。

docs.title: 2. ​在​ Kruskal ​算法​中​怎么​用​？
docs.text:
Kruskal ​的​过程​就是​不断​选边​的​过程​。​假设​我们​要​考虑​边​ \((u, v)\)：

  1. ​查验​ (Find):

    • ​算​一下​ root_u = Find(u)

    • ​算​一下​ root_v = Find(v)

  2. ​判断​环​ (Cycle Detection):

    • ​如果​ root_u == root_v：​说明​ \(u\) ​和​ \(v\) ​已经​是​同一个​帮派​的​了​（​也就是说​，​它们​之间​早就​有​一条​路​连通​了​）。

    • ​决策​： ​千万​不能​加​这​条边​！​如果​加​了​，​就​会​形成​环​ (Cycle)。​这条​边​是​多余​的​，​扔掉​。

  3. ​合并​ (Union):

    • ​如果​ root_u != root_v：​说明​这是​两个​互​不相干​的​孤岛​。

    • ​决策​： ​加​这​条边​！​这是​连接​两个​世界​的​桥梁​。​然后​执行​ Union(u, v)，​把​这​两个​岛​合并​成​一个​大岛​。

docs.title: 4. ​为什么​它​极快​？(Path Compression)
docs.text:
Union-Find ​有​一个​极其​聪明​的​优化​叫​ “​路径​压缩​” (Path Compression)。

  • ​问题​： ​如果​合并​了​很​多次​，​层级​关系​可能​会​变成​一条​长长的​链​：A \(\to\) B \(\to\) C \(\to\) D... \(\to\) Z。​每次​问​ A ​老大​是​谁​，​都​要​跑断腿​问​到​ Z。

  • ​优化​： ​当​ A ​第一次​费劲​找到​大​ Boss Z ​之后​，​他会​直接​告诉​自己​：“​去​他​的​中间商​，​我​以后​直接​认​ Z ​做​老大​。”

  • ​结果​： ​树​的​高度​被​压得​非常​扁​。

​因为​这个​优化​，Union-Find ​的​操作​几乎​是​ \(O(1)\) (​常数​时间​)。​这​就是​为什么​ Kruskal ​算法​处理​稀疏​图​非常​快​的​原因​。

​总结​：

  • Union-Find ​就是​用来​快速​回答​ “​它​俩​连通​了​吗​？” ​这个​问题​的​数据结构​。

  • ​如果​连通​了​ \(\rightarrow\) ​不要​加边​（​会成​环​）。

  • ​如果​不​连通​ \(\rightarrow\) ​加边​，​并​合并​。

docs.title: Subtopics
docs.text:
• Marginalization — sum/integrate out variables: p(x) = Σ_y p(x,y) (discrete) / ∫ p(x,y)dy
  • Product rule — p(x,y) = p(x|y)p(y) = p(y|x)p(x)
  • Bayes' rule — p(y|x) = p(x|y)p(y) / p(x)
  • Total probability — p(x) = Σ_y p(x|y)p(y)
  • Independence — X ⊥ Y ⇔ p(x,y) = p(x)p(y)
  • iid — p(x₁…xₙ|θ) = Π p(xᵢ|θ)
  • Moments — E[Xᵏ] k-th moment; 1st = mean, 2nd = spread
  • Variance Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]²; Covariance Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]

docs.title: Three things that trip people up
docs.text:
• Densities are not point probabilities: f_x(x); P(a≤X≤b) = ∫ f_x(x)dx
  • Linearity of expectation E[X]+E[Y]=E[X+Y] is always true; E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] needs independence
  • If X,Y uncorrelated → Cov=0 but not necessarily independent (except under joint Gaussian)

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
Does uncorrelated imply independent?

No. Cov(X,Y)=0 means no linear dependence. They can still be dependent non-linearly. The exception: jointly Gaussian variables — there uncorrelated ⇒ independent.

docs.title: Subtopics
docs.text:
• Likelihood L(θ) = P(D|θ) (function of model params)
  • MLE θ_MLE = argmax_θ P(D|θ)
  • Log-likelihood ℓ(θ) = log L(θ) = Σ log P(xᵢ|θ) (because log(ab)=log a + log b)
  • Common MLE results: categorical θ̂_k = n_k/n; Gaussian μ̂ = (1/N)Σxᵢ
  • Posterior & MAP P(θ|D) = P(D|θ)P(θ)/P(D); θ_MAP = argmax_θ P(D|θ)P(θ)

docs.title: Derivations to be able to reproduce
docs.text:
• Derive θ̂_k = n_k/n for categorical data (Lagrange multiplier with Σθ_k=1 constraint)
  • Derive μ̂ = (1/N)Σxᵢ for Gaussian data (set dℓ/dμ = 0)

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
MLE vs MAP — relationship?

MLE maximizes P(D|θ). MAP maximizes P(θ|D) ∝ P(D|θ)·P(θ), adding a prior that acts like regularization. MAP → MLE as data grows or the prior becomes uniform.

Why maximize log-likelihood instead of likelihood?

Products of many small probabilities underflow; log turns the product into a sum (Σ log p), which is numerically stable and easier to differentiate. argmax is unchanged since log is monotonic.

docs.title: Linear Regression & Regularization
docs.text: Design matrix: each row = a data point, each column = a feature. A ∈ ℝ^{n×d}, w ∈ ℝ^d, ŷ = Aw.

docs.title: Subtopics
docs.text:
• OLS (ordinary least squares) min ‖y − Aw‖²; linear model + Gaussian noise → squared loss
  • Normal equation: AᵀA w = Aᵀy → if AᵀA invertible, ŵ = (AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀy
  • Ridge (L2 penalty) min ‖y−Aw‖² + λ‖w‖² → ŵ = (AᵀA + λI)⁻¹Aᵀy (shrinks weights, keeps all features)
  • Lasso (L1 penalty) min ‖y−Aw‖² + λ‖w‖₁ (feature selection, creates sparsity)
  • Gaussian noise view → recovers MLE / OLS
  • Gaussian prior view → regularization from MAP (w ~ N(0,τ²) ⇒ Ridge, λ ∝ σ²/τ²)

docs.title: n vs d
docs.text:
• n > d: overdetermined, AᵀA invertible, low variance, possible bias
  • n < d: underdetermined, not invertible, zero training error, high variance
  • Ridge solves it: (AᵀA + λI)⁻¹ always invertible for λ > 0

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
L1 vs L2 regularization — what's the practical difference?

L2 (Ridge) shrinks weights smoothly, keeps all features, handles correlated features. L1 (Lasso) drives some weights to exactly 0 → sparse, automatic feature selection. ElasticNet blends both.

How does Ridge fix non-invertibility?

Adding λI makes (AᵀA + λI) positive-definite and thus always invertible for λ>0, so a unique solution exists even when n<d or features are collinear.

docs.title: Clustering
docs.text: Clustering = grouping without labels (unsupervised).

docs.title: Subtopics
docs.text:
• k-means — choose cluster centers + assignments so each point is close to its assigned center. Hard assignment. Objective min Σᵢ ‖xᵢ − c_{aᵢ}‖²
  • k-means++ initialization; choosing k; limitations (assumes spherical, sensitive to init/outliers)
  • Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) — each point generated by one hidden Gaussian component. Soft assignment. P(z=k)=π_k, x|z=k ~ N(μ_k, Σ_k)
  • Per-point likelihood P(x) = Σ_k π_k N(x|μ_k,Σ_k)
  • Dataset log-likelihood ℓ(θ) = Σᵢ log[ Σ_k π_k N(xᵢ|μ_k,Σ_k) ]
  • EM intuition (E: responsibilities, M: update params) — GMM is solved with EM

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
k-means vs GMM?

k-means = hard assignment, spherical equal-size clusters, minimizes within-cluster squared distance. GMM = soft (probabilistic) assignment via mixture of Gaussians, can model elliptical clusters and uncertainty; fit with EM. k-means is essentially GMM with hard assignment + shared spherical covariance.

How do you pick k?

Elbow method (within-cluster SSE vs k), silhouette score, or for GMM use BIC/AIC. Often domain knowledge.

docs.title: Generative — "how would each class generate this data?" P(x|y)·P(y)
docs.text:
• GDA (Gaussian Discriminant Analysis) ŷ = argmax_k [log P(x|y=k) + log P(y=k)]
  • LDA — P(x|y=k)=N(μ_k, Σ), all classes share covariance Σ → linear decision boundary
  • QDA — P(x|y=k)=N(μ_k, Σ_k), per-class covariance → quadratic boundary
  • LDA & QDA are two kinds of GDA
  • Naive Bayes — treats features independent given class: P(x|y=k)=Π_j P(x_j|y=k)

docs.title: Discriminative — "given this data, what class is it?" P(y|x)
docs.text:
• Logistic regression P(y=1|x,w)=σ(wᵀx), σ(z)=1/(1+e⁻ᶻ) ∈(0,1)
  • Bernoulli likelihood → log loss ℓ(w)=Σ[yᵢ log pᵢ + (1−yᵢ)log(1−pᵢ)]; no closed-form MLE
  • Softmax regression P(y=k|x)=e^{w_kᵀx}/Σ_j e^{w_jᵀx} (multi-class); boundary a vs b is linear
  • Metrics: TPR=TP/(TP+FN), FPR=FP/(FP+TN), Precision=TP/(TP+FP)

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
Generative vs discriminative classifier?

Generative models P(x|y)P(y) (the data-generating process), then uses Bayes to get P(y|x) — e.g. GDA, Naive Bayes. Discriminative models P(y|x) directly — e.g. logistic/softmax regression. Discriminative usually wins with lots of data; generative can do better with little data / handle missing features.

Why is LDA's boundary linear but QDA's quadratic?

LDA assumes a shared covariance Σ across classes; the quadratic terms cancel, leaving a linear boundary. QDA allows per-class Σ_k, so the quadratic terms survive.

Why no closed-form for logistic regression?

The log-loss with a sigmoid is non-linear in w; setting the gradient to 0 has no analytic solution, so it's solved iteratively (gradient descent / Newton's method).

docs.title: Subtopics
docs.text:
• Gradient descent w_{t+1} = w_t − η∇J(w_t) — η (learning rate) controls step size; "moving downhill"
  • Taylor approximation J(w+δ) ≈ J(w) + ∇J(w)ᵀδ + ½δᵀHδ — justifies GD with δ = −η∇J(w); H = Hessian (why η matters)
  • Quadratic eigen-coordinate update α_{t+1} = (1−ηλᵢ)α_t; convergence needs |1−ηλᵢ|<1 ⇒ 0<η<2/λ_max
  • SGD unbiasedness E_i[∇Lᵢ(w)] = ∇J(w) — single-sample gradient is unbiased estimate of full gradient
  • SGD is unbiased but noisy; η too large can diverge

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
Why does the learning rate matter (link to the Hessian)?

The Taylor expansion shows convergence in each eigen-direction is governed by (1−ηλ). Stability needs η < 2/λ_max. Too large → oscillate/diverge; too small → slow. The Hessian's eigenvalue spread (condition number) determines how hard the problem is.

Batch GD vs SGD vs mini-batch?

Batch: exact gradient on all data, stable but slow/memory-heavy. SGD: one sample, unbiased but noisy, fast updates, can escape shallow minima. Mini-batch: best of both, the practical default; noise also has a mild regularizing effect.

docs.title: NN basics
docs.text:
• Pre-activation zₗ = Wₗ hₗ₋₁ + bₗ; non-linear activation hₗ = φ(zₗ)
  • ReLU φ(z)=max(0,z) — solves vanishing gradients from sigmoid, but "dead neurons" (z<0 → grad 0)
  • Universal Approximation Theorem — existence result: one hidden layer can approximate any function
  • Large width → many neurons → many piecewise-linear segments (via ReLU)
  • Depth → feature reuse: simple → mid → complex features

docs.title: Debugging with loss curves
docs.text:
• Underfitting: train loss high, val loss high, bias high, variance low
  • Overfitting: train loss low, val loss high, bias low, variance high
  • Optimization issues (how we fit): loss diverges if η too large; plateaus → poor init
  • Capacity control = how complex the model is allowed to be
  • Fixes: ① reduce parameters / early stopping; ② regularization L_reg = J + λ‖w‖²
  • Weight decay: w ← w − η(∂J/∂w + λw) = (1−ηλ)w − η∂J/∂w

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
Why ReLU over sigmoid?

Sigmoid saturates → tiny gradients → vanishing gradients in deep nets. ReLU has gradient 1 for z>0, so gradients flow; it's also cheap. Downside: dead neurons (z<0 → 0 gradient); fixed by Leaky/ELU/GELU.

Read a loss curve: train low, val high — what is it and how to fix?

Overfitting (high variance). Fix with more data, regularization (L2/dropout), early stopping, or a smaller model.

What does weight decay do, mechanically?

L2 regularization adds λ‖w‖²; its gradient shrinks weights toward 0 each step (w ← (1−ηλ)w − η∂J/∂w), discouraging large weights and reducing variance.

docs.title: CNN pipeline
docs.text:
• Input: raw image → Conv+ReLU (slide filter, Σ w·x + b → feature map) → Pooling (max pool, halves spatial size, downsampling) → Conv+ReLU (high-level patterns) → flatten → z = Wz → class scores → softmax
  • Output shape H_out = ⌊(H + 2P − F)/S⌋ + 1 — F=kernel size, P=padding, S=stride
  • Receptive field R_ℓ = R_{ℓ−1} + (k_ℓ − 1)·Π_{j<ℓ} S_j (init R₀=1)
  • Counting parameters: conv layer (k×k×C_in + 1)·C_out; fully connected input_size × output_size + bias
  • Inductive biases: translation equivariance, locality (nearby pixels correlated), parameter efficiency
  • ViT (Vision Transformer): split image into p×p patches → flatten → linear project → tokens. No inductive bias, global receptive field, needs more data

docs.title: ResNet
docs.text:
• Why ResNet? Deep nets suffer ① vanishing gradients (early-layer derivatives tiny → slow update) and ② accuracy degradation (deeper → higher training error). Skip connections fix degradation
  • Residual: learn the difference between desired output and input, y = F(x) + x (F learned by weighted layers)
  • Gradient flows through identity: ∂L/∂x = ∂L/∂y·(∂F/∂x + 1) — the +1 keeps gradient alive
  • Architecture: x → Conv → BatchNorm → ReLU → Conv → BatchNorm, then y = ReLU(F(x)+x)
  • BatchNorm: per mini-batch, normalize with batch mean/variance (μ_B, σ_B²)

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
What problem do residual connections actually solve?

The degradation problem: plain deep nets get higher training error than shallower ones (an optimization issue, not overfitting). The identity skip lets a block learn a residual F(x) and passes gradient through unchanged (the +1 term), making very deep nets trainable.

CNN vs ViT inductive bias?

CNNs bake in locality + translation equivariance + parameter sharing, so they're data-efficient. ViTs have almost no spatial inductive bias (global attention over patches) → more flexible but need much more data (or pretraining) to match CNNs.

docs.title: Attention mechanics
docs.text:
• Core question: "for this token, which other tokens should I attend to?"
  • Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k) V — scaled dot-product attention
  • Per token: Query q (what I'm looking for), Key k (what I offer for matching), Value v (the info I contribute)
  • Step 1 embedding + positional: x_pos = x + PE; then qᵢ=xᵢW_q, kᵢ=xᵢW_k, vᵢ=xᵢW_v
  • QKᵀ = query-key similarity; /√d_k scales so scores don't get too big (keeps softmax stable)
  • softmax → attention weights (probabilities); multiply V → weighted sum of values
  • Shapes: Q,K,V ∈ ℝ^{n×d}; QKᵀ ∈ ℝ^{n×n}; output ℝ^{n×d}

docs.title: Self vs cross attention
docs.text:
• Self-attention: Q,K,V from the same sequence. Encoder = unmasked/bidirectional; Decoder = masked (causal, look-ahead mask sets future scores to −∞ → 0 after softmax)
  • Cross-attention: Q from decoder hidden states, K,V from encoder output (one sequence attends to another)
  • Positional encoding: PE(pos,2i)=sin(pos/10000^{2i/d}), PE(pos,2i+1)=cos(...). Absolute vs relative; RoPE = rotary, better extrapolation / longer context
  • Multi-head attention: each head learns a different relationship; head_i=Attention(Q_i,K_i,V_i); concat heads; d_k = d_model/num_heads

docs.title: Encoder / Decoder layer
docs.text:
• Encoder layer: x → multi-head self-attn → add residual → LayerNorm → FFN → add residual → LayerNorm
  • FFN FFN(X)=G(XW₁+b₁)W₂+b₂, G=ReLU/GeLU; larger hidden = more room to learn
  • Decoder layer: masked multi-head self-attn → Add&Norm → cross-attn → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
Why divide by √d_k in attention?

Dot products of d_k-dim vectors grow with d_k, pushing softmax into saturated regions with tiny gradients. Dividing by √d_k keeps the variance ~1 so softmax stays well-conditioned.

Why multi-head instead of one big attention?

Each head can attend to a different relationship/subspace (e.g. syntax vs co-reference) at lower per-head dimension; concatenating them gives a richer representation than a single head of the same total size.

What does the causal mask do in the decoder?

It sets attention scores for future positions to −∞ (→ 0 after softmax) so each token can only attend to itself and earlier tokens, preserving autoregressive generation.

docs.title: Subtopics
docs.text:
• Sequence modeling P(x₁…x_T) = Π P(xₜ|x_{<t}); model P_θ(x_{1:T}) approximates true
  • Training objective: MLE = minimizing negative log-likelihood L = −Σ log P_θ(xₜ|x_{<t}) ⇔ cross-entropy loss
  • Teacher forcing: model conditions on ground-truth tokens during training
  • Decoding (decoder = causal masking = autoregressive):
  • Greedy: deterministic, pick most probable; repetitive
  • Temperature sampling: pᵢ ∝ exp(zᵢ/T); T→0 concentrates (greedy), T→∞ uniform (diverse)
  • Top-k: restrict to k most probable tokens
  • Top-p (nucleus): smallest set whose cumulative prob ≥ p (adaptive count)
  • KV-caching: store past keys/values; at step k only compute Q/K/V for the new token. Trades memory for speed (O(N³)→O(N²) attention over the sequence)
  • Fine-tuning ladder:
  • SFT — train on same cross-entropy objective as pretraining
  • LoRA — freeze base weights W, add low-rank update W = W₀ + AB, A∈ℝ^{d×r}, B∈ℝ^{r×d}; works because ΔW has low intrinsic dimensionality
  • RLHF — reward model + RL
  • DPO — directly optimize preference pairs (no separate reward model)

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
Greedy vs temperature vs top-k vs top-p decoding?

Greedy: always the argmax — deterministic, repetitive. Temperature reshapes the distribution (low=sharp, high=flat). Top-k samples from the k most likely tokens. Top-p (nucleus) samples from the smallest set covering probability p, adapting the candidate count to model confidence.

Why does LoRA work / what does it save?

Fine-tuning updates lie in a low-rank subspace, so ΔW ≈ AB with small rank r. You train only A,B (tiny fraction of params), freezing the base model — far less memory and storage, and swappable adapters.

What does KV-caching speed up and how?

Autoregressive generation recomputes attention over all previous tokens each step. Caching past K/V means each new step only computes K/V for the single new token, cutting redundant work (memory cost for compute savings).

docs.title: Self-Supervised Learning
docs.text: Learn representations from unlabeled data via a pretext task.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Input Pretext task Gen/Disc Loss Autoencoder Image Reconstruct the input (z=f_θ(x), x̂=g_θ(z)) Generative MSE ‖x−x̂‖² Context Encoder Masked image Predict the masked region Generative MSE + Adversarial Image Rotation Rotated image Predict rotation (0/90/180/270°) Discriminative Cross-entropy SimCLR (Contrastive) Two images Are they augmentations of the same image? Discriminative Contrastive

docs.title: Key ideas
docs.text:
• Autoencoder: understand global structure, must infer corrupted part; sparse/regularized variants
  • Adversarial loss: generator creates fake images for discriminator → wants D to say "real"; discriminator classifies real vs fake
  • Contrastive loss: bring similar points together, push dissimilar apart. Temperature T: small T = hardest negatives, large T = smoother

docs.title: Interview Q&A
docs.text:
What is a "pretext task" in self-supervised learning?

A task whose labels come free from the data itself (predict rotation, reconstruct masked region, match augmentations). Solving it forces the model to learn useful representations transferable to downstream tasks.

How does contrastive learning (SimCLR) work?

Take two augmentations of the same image (positive pair) and other images (negatives). The contrastive loss pulls positive pairs' embeddings together and pushes negatives apart, learning representations invariant to augmentations.

docs.title: Networks
docs.text:
Eighteen notes on random graphs, and about thirty methods holding them together. The notes are the details; this page is the shape.

Most of these topics are the same few ideas wearing different clothes. A branching process explains the giant component, then explains an epidemic, then explains the bow-tie in a directed graph. Concentration is how you turn "on average" into "almost surely" everywhere it appears. The tables below are an attempt to make that reuse visible: which method powers which topic, and which question shape reaches for what.

Start with the decision tree if you have a problem in front of you, or the topic index if you want the map.

Topics in scope (18 notes): Thresholds, Concentration, Branching Processes, Expected Counts & Union Bounds, Graph Counting, Configuration Model, Preferential Attachment, SIR & Epidemics, Giant Component, Diameter of Giant, SIR on Networks, Small World, Directed Networks, SBM & Multitype BP, Markov Chains & PageRank, Mixing Time & Spectral Gap, Spectral Clustering, Information Cascades.

docs.title: 3. Decision tree by question type
docs.text:
What kind of question is this?
│
├── "Show structure doesn't exist w.h.p."         → 1st moment   → Thresholds / Expected Counts
├── "Show structure exists w.h.p."                → 2nd moment   → Thresholds / Expected Counts
├── "X is close to its mean w.h.p."
│   ├── Variance available?                       → Chebyshev    → Concentration
│   ├── i.i.d. sum?                               → Chernov      → Concentration
│   └── Tail bound *relative to* $E[X]$?          → Mult Chernov → Concentration
│
├── "Survival/extinction probability"
│   ├── Single type?                              → $G(\eta)=\eta$, smallest root → BP / SIR
│   └── Multiple types (SBM)?                     → $\vec\eta=\vec G(\vec\eta)$,
│                                                   $\lambda_{\max}(C)>1$ → SBM
│
├── "Quantity evolves over time"
│   ├── Discrete, one step at a time              → Conditional E + recurrence → PA
│   └── Large scale, continuous limit             → ODE / fluid limit → CM, Giant
│
├── "Size of giant / outbreak"
│   ├── ER $G(n,c/n)$                            → BFS sandwich → Giant Component
│   ├── ER + SIR ($R_0=c\bar p$)                 → Two-layer Chernov → SIR on Networks
│   └── CM + SIR                                 → $\theta$-reduction ODE → SIR on Networks
│
├── "How fast does the chain mix?"
│   ├── Have $\lambda_2$ / $\Lambda_2$?          → spectral bound → Mixing Time
│   ├── Have a bottleneck cut?                   → Cheeger ($\phi$) → Mixing Time
│   └── PageRank with restart?                   → coupling, $(1-\alpha)^t$ → MC & PageRank
│
├── "Find communities / partition"
│   ├── Two blocks, planted                      → check $ab>\varepsilon^2$ → Spectral Clustering
│   └── $k$-way clustering                       → top-$d$ eigvecs + $k$-means → Spectral Clustering
│
├── "Choose $k$ seeds, max spread"               → submodular + greedy → Info Cascades
│
└── "Probability of specific multigraph in CM"   → matching indicators + counting → Config Model

docs.title: Random graph thresholds
docs.text:
• Isolated vertices: \(p^* = \log n / n\)
  • Connectivity: same threshold, \(p^* = \log n / n\)
  • Cycles appear: \(p^* = 1/n\)
  • Giant component: \(c = 1\), i.e. \(p^* = 1/n\)
  • Diameter: \(\Theta(\log n / \log(np))\)

docs.title: Counting
docs.text:
• Unoriented \(k\)-cycles on \(k\) labeled vertices: \(\frac{(k-1)!}{2}\)
  • Labeled trees on \(k\) vertices (Cayley): \(k^{k-2}\)
  • Perfect matchings of \(M\) items: \((M-1)!!\)
  • \(\binom{n}{k} \le n^k/k!\), \(k^k \le k! e^{k-1}\)

docs.title: Branching / SIR
docs.text:
• Poisson BP PGF: \(G(\lambda) = e^{-c(1-\lambda)}\), extinction \(\eta + e^{-c(1-\eta)}=\eta\) ⇒ \(\theta + e^{-c\theta}=1\)
  • SIR limiting PGF: \(G_c(\lambda) = \frac{1}{1+c(1-\lambda)}\), extinction \(\eta = 1/c\), survival \(P = 1 - 1/c\)
  • Subcritical SIR tail: \(\Pr(|T_\text{SIR}|>k) \le e^{-(1-c)^2 k/4}\)
  • Rate function: \(I(x) \ge \frac{(x-c)^2}{2x(1+x)}\) for \(c\le x\le 1\)

docs.title: Multiplicative Chernov
docs.text:
• \(\Pr(S_k \ge (1+\epsilon) E S_k) \le e^{-\epsilon^2 E S_k / (2(1+\epsilon))}\)
  • \(\Pr(S_k \le (1-\epsilon) E S_k) \le e^{-\epsilon^2 E S_k / 2}\)

docs.title: Giant component / SIR on \(G(n,p)\)
docs.text:
• \(G(n,c/n)\): \(|C(x)|/n \to \theta\), \(\theta+e^{-c\theta}=1\)
  • SIR on \(G(n,p)\) with recovery \(T\): \(R_0 = c\bar p\), \(\bar p = E[1-e^{-\beta T}]\), \(\theta+e^{-R_0\theta}=1\)
  • Fluid limit: \(f(x) = 1 - e^{-cx} - x\), \(f_{\pm\epsilon}\) sandwich

docs.title: Configuration model
docs.text:
• \(E[m_{\alpha,\beta}] = 1/(\ell_n-1)\), disjoint pairs: \(1/[(\ell_n-1)(\ell_n-3)]\)
  • \(E[\text{loops}] = \frac{1}{\ell_n-1}\sum_i\binom{d_i}{2}\)
  • Graph prob: \(P(G) = \frac{1}{(\ell_n-1)!!}\prod_i\frac{d_i!}{2^{A_{ii}}A_{ii}!}\prod_{i<j}\frac{1}{A_{ij}!}\)

docs.title: Preferential attachment
docs.text: • \(E[D_i(t)] \approx D_i(i)\sqrt{t/i}\) (after solving \(\prod(1+1/(2s))\))

docs.title: Random walks / mixing
docs.text:
• Stationary on \(G\): \(\pi_v = d_v/(2|E|)\)
  • Symmetrized: \(\tilde P = D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\)
  • PageRank coupling: \(\|\mu_{t,x} - \pi\|_{TV} \le (1-\alpha)^t \le e^{-\alpha t}\)
  • Overlap-method: if \(\min M_{ij} \ge \epsilon\), \(d_{TV}(\mu_t, \pi) \le (1-\epsilon n)^t\)
  • Cheeger: \(\phi^2/8 \le 1-\lambda_2 \le \phi\)
  • Lazy mixing: \(\tilde\lambda_2^t \le \bar d(t) \le \tilde\lambda_2^t / \sqrt{\pi_{\min}}\)
  • Submultiplicativity: \(\tilde d(t+s) \le \tilde d(t) \tilde d(s)\), \(d(k\tau) \le e^{-k}\)
  • Mixing time: \(\tau \asymp \frac{1}{1-\lambda_2}\log(1/\pi_{\min})\)
  • TV three forms: \(d_{TV}(\mu, \pi) = \sup_S |\mu(S)-\pi(S)| = \tfrac12 \|\mu-\pi\|_1 = \sup_f |E_\mu f - E_\pi f|/\delta(f)\)

docs.title: Diameter (Giant Component)
docs.text:
• \(G(n, c/n)\): \(D / \log_c n o_P 1\) for \(c > 1\)
  • CM: \(D / \log_ u n o_P 1\) where \( u = E[D(D-1)]/E[D]\) (excess degree)
  • Newman-Watts (cycle + \(G(n, c/n)\)): \(D / \log_ u n o 1\), \( u = frac12[c+1+\sqrt{(c+1)^2+4c(2k-1)}]\)

docs.title: Small world (Kleinberg)
docs.text:
• 1D ring + random long edges: greedy takes \(\Theta(\sqrt n)\) steps (no algo does better)
  • \(d\)-D grid + long edges \(\propto 1/d^\alpha\): greedy is \(\Theta((\log n)^2)\) iff \(\alpha = d\), else \(n^c\)

docs.title: Directed networks (bow-tie)
docs.text:
• \(D(n, c/n)\), \(c > 1\): SCC core size \(\theta^2 n\), in/out fans \(\theta(1-\theta) n\), rest \((1-\theta)^2 n\)
  • Where \(\theta\) = survival prob of \(\text{Poi}(c)\) BP (same as undirected giant)

docs.title: Configuration model — simplicity (Lecture 11)
docs.text:
• \( u = E[D(D-1)]/E[D]\)
  • \(\# ext{loops} o ext{Poi}( u/2)\), \(\# ext{multi-edges} o ext{Poi}( u^2/4)\)
  • \(\Pr(G ext{ simple}) o e^{- u/2 - u^2/4}\)
  • Chung-Lu: \(p_{ij} = w_i w_j / \ell_n\), giant exists iff \( ilde c = \sum w_i^2 / \sum w_i > 1\)

docs.title: Spectral clustering
docs.text:
• Generalized eigenproblem: \((D-A)\psi = \Lambda D\psi\), \(\Lambda = 1-\lambda\)
  • Variational \(\Lambda_2\): \(\min_{\psi^T D\mathbf 1 = 0} \frac{\psi^T(D-A)\psi}{\psi^T D\psi}\)
  • Two-block recovery: \(ab > \varepsilon^2\)

docs.title: Influence max
docs.text:
• IC influence: \(I(A) = E_X|C_X(A)|\) (random digraph reduction)
  • Greedy: \(f(A_k) \ge (1-1/e)\,\text{OPT}\)

docs.title: Multitype BP / SBM
docs.text:
• Mean matrix \(C_{k\ell} = B_{k\ell}\alpha_\ell\)
  • Criticality: \(\lambda_{\max}(C) > 1\) ⇔ giant exists
  • Survival: \(\theta = \sum_i \alpha_i\theta_i\), \(\theta_i = 1 - e^{-\sum_j C_{ij}\theta_j}\) (SBM Poisson)

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: Counting specific substructures (cycles, trees, matchings, wedges) in random graphs. These counting arguments underpin almost every expected-count calculation. The main challenge is counting correctly without overcounting due to symmetries (orientations, starting points, labelings).

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Labeled trees on \(k\) vertices: \(k^{k-2}\) (Cayley's formula)

Unoriented cycles on \(k\) labeled vertices: \(\frac{(k-1)!}{2}\) - Start with \(k!\) permutations, divide by \(k\) (rotations) and \(2\) (reflections)

Wedges (paths of length 2): Choose center vertex, then 2 neighbors. Total in \(K_n\): \(n\binom{n-1}{2} = 3\binom{n}{3}\)

Perfect matchings of \(M\) elements: \((M-1)!! = (M-1)(M-3)\cdots 1\)

Bipartite matchings of two sets of size \(M\): \(M!\)

Binomial bound: \(\binom{n}{k} \leq \frac{n^k}{k!}\)

Inductive bound: \(k^k \leq k! \cdot e^{k-1}\) (proved using \((1+1/k)^k \leq e\))

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Start with labeled count, then divide out symmetries
  2. For cycles: \(k!/2k = (k-1)!/2\) (remove starting point and orientation)
  3. For trees: use Cayley's formula \(k^{k-2}\)
  4. For matchings: sequential algorithm gives \((M-1)!!\)
  5. Bound binomial coefficients: \(\binom{n}{k} \leq n^k/k!\)

docs.title: Cycle counting
docs.text:
\((k-1)!/2\) unoriented cycles on \(k\) labeled vertices.

Derivation: \(k!\) permutations \(\to\) divide by \(k\) (cyclic rotations give same cycle) \(\to\) divide by \(2\) (two orientations) \(= (k-1)!/2\).

docs.title: Tree counting (Cayley's formula)
docs.text:
\(k^{k-2}\) labeled trees on \(k\) vertices.

Useful bound: \(k^{k-2} \cdot p^{k-1} \leq \frac{e^{k-1}}{k^2} \cdot p^{k-1}\) after using \(k^k \leq k! e^{k-1}\).

docs.title: Perfect matching count
docs.text:
\((M-1)!!\) perfect matchings of \(M\) elements.

Algorithm proof: Pick lowest element, pair with one of \(M-1\) others, remove pair, repeat. Choices: \((M-1)(M-3)\cdots 1\).

Key identity: \(m! \cdot 2^m \cdot (2m-1)!! = (2m)!\)

docs.title: Overlap counting for variance
docs.text: When computing \(E[N_w^2]\), classify pairs of wedges by edge overlap: - 2 shared edges: Same wedge. Count: \(E[N_w]\) terms, probability \(p^2\) each. - 1 shared edge, same vertices: 2 choices per wedge. - 1 shared edge, different vertices: New vertex from \(n-3\) options, 4 ways to connect. Total: \(n(n-1)(n-2)(2n-5)\) pairs, probability \(p^3\) each. - 0 shared edges: At most \((E[N_w]/p^2)^2\) pairs, probability \(p^4\) each.

docs.title: Configuration model graph probability
docs.text: $\(N(G) = \prod_i \frac{d_i!}{2^{A_{ii}} A_{ii}!} \cdot \prod_{i<j} \frac{1}{A_{ij}!}\)$ (Proof via decentralized algorithm — each vertex independently chooses local matchings.)

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting to divide by 2 for cycle orientation
  • Using \(k!\) instead of \((k-1)!/2\) for cycle count
  • Not proving the sequential algorithm gives a bijection (each matching maps to exactly one sequence of choices)
  • In overlap counting: missing a case or miscounting the number of pairs in each case
  • Confusing labeled vs unlabeled counts

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Cycles: \(k!\) permutations, divide by \(2k\) = \((k-1)!/2\)"
  • "Trees: \(k^{k-2}\) (Cayley's formula)"
  • "Matchings: sequential pairing gives \((M-1)!!\)"
  • "When in doubt about overcounting: think about what symmetry maps one description to another"

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: Many proofs in random graph theory start by counting how many copies of some structure (cycles, isolated vertices, bad subgraphs, multi-edges) exist, then using expectation bounds to show the count is zero or nonzero w.h.p. The expected count method (first moment method) and the union bound are the simplest and most widely used tools in this course.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Indicator sum: \(X = \sum_{i \in S} I_i\) where \(I_i = 1\) if structure \(i\) appears.

First moment method: \(\Pr(X > 0) \leq E[X]\). If \(E[X] \to 0\), no structure exists w.h.p.

Union bound: \(\Pr(\bigcup A_i) \leq \sum \Pr(A_i)\). (Same as first moment when \(X = \sum I_i\).)

Second moment method: \(\Pr(X > 0) \geq \frac{(E[X])^2}{E[X^2]}\). Proves existence when the count concentrates.

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Define \(X = \sum I_i\) — identify all candidate structures
  2. Count candidates — how many possible locations for the structure?
  3. Compute per-candidate probability — probability each candidate actually appears
  4. Multiply: \(E[X] = (\text{number of candidates}) \times (\text{probability per candidate})\)
  5. If second moment needed: compute \(E[X^2]\) by splitting into overlap cases

docs.title: Expected number of cycles
docs.text:
Statement: Expected number of \(k\)-cycles in \(G(n,p)\): $\(E[Y_k] = \binom{n}{k} \frac{(k-1)!}{2} p^k \leq (np)^k\)$

Why it matters: Shows \(G(n,p)\) has no cycles when \(p \ll 1/n\).

Proof: - Candidates: \(\binom{n}{k}\) ways to choose \(k\) vertices, \(\frac{(k-1)!}{2}\) unoriented cycles on them - Probability: \(p^k\) (all \(k\) edges present) - Total: \(\sum_{k \geq 3} E[Y_k] \leq \frac{(np)^3}{1-np} \to 0\) when \(np \to 0\)

docs.title: Expected number of trees of size \(k\)
docs.text:
Statement: \(E[X_k] = \binom{n}{k} k^{k-2} p^{k-1}\)

Useful bound: \(k^k \leq k! e^{k-1}\) (by induction using \((1+1/k)^k \leq e\)), giving: $\(E[X_k] \leq \frac{1}{epk^2}(npe)^k\)$

Aggregate bound: \(E[X_{\geq k}] \leq pn^2 (npe)^{k-2}\), which is small for \(k \geq 2 + \frac{\log n}{\lambda_n}\) where \(\lambda_n = -\log(npe)\).

docs.title: Expected self-loops in configuration model
docs.text: Statement: Expected loops = \(\frac{1}{\ell_n - 1} \sum_i \binom{d_i}{2}\)

docs.title: Expected multi-edges in configuration model
docs.text:
Statement: $\(\sum_{\{i,j\}} \Pr(A_{ij} \geq 2) \leq \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i \neq j} \frac{d_i(d_i-1) d_j(d_j-1)}{(\ell_n-1)(\ell_n-3)}\)$

Proof idea: Bound \(1[A_{ij} \geq 2] \leq \frac{1}{2}A_{ij}(A_{ij}-1)\), expand using matching indicators, use disjoint pair probability.

docs.title: Wedge and triangle counts
docs.text: Statement: - Expected wedges: \(E[N_w] = 3\binom{n}{3}p^2 = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{2}p^2\) - Expected triangles: \(E[N_\Delta] = \binom{n}{3}p^3\) - Global clustering: \(C_{\text{global}} \approx \frac{3E[N_\Delta]}{E[N_w]} = p\)

docs.title: Variance via overlap counting
docs.text:
Statement: \(E[N_w^2]\) splits into three cases by edge overlap: - 2 shared edges (same wedge): contributes \(E[N_w] \cdot p^2\) (wait, it's just \(E[N_w]\) since same wedge) - 1 shared edge: \(n(n-1)(n-2)(2n-5)\) pairs, each needing \(p^3\) - 0 shared edges: at most \((E[N_w])^2 / p^4 \cdot p^4 = (E[N_w])^2\)

Result: \(\text{Var}(N_w) \leq 9\left(\frac{1}{n^{3/2}p} + \frac{1}{n^3 p^2}\right)(E[N_w])^2\)

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Overcounting cycles: forgetting to divide by \(2k\) (orientations and starting points) — result is \(\frac{(k-1)!}{2}\)
  • Not bounding \(\binom{n}{k}\) correctly: use \(\binom{n}{k} \leq \frac{n^k}{k!}\) for upper bounds
  • In variance calculations: missing an overlap case or miscounting the number of pairs with that overlap
  • Using \(E[X^2] = (E[X])^2\) (that's only true for constants!) — you need to actually compute \(E[X^2]\)
  • In the tree size argument: forgetting that \(E[X_{\geq k}]\) needs a geometric series sum, not just one term

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Count candidates, multiply by probability, sum — that's the expected count"
  • "\(\frac{(k-1)!}{2}\) for unoriented cycles: divide by \(k\) starting points and \(2\) orientations"
  • "Variance = how pairs of structures correlate, which depends on how much they overlap"
  • "Union bound is just first moment in disguise"

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: Concentration inequalities let you upgrade "in expectation" results to "with high probability" results. When a problem says "show that \(X\) is close to its mean w.h.p." or "with probability tending to 1," you need concentration. The main tools in this course are Markov, Chebyshev, and the large deviation / rate function bound.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Markov's inequality: For non-negative \(X\) and \(a > 0\): $\(\Pr(X \geq a) \leq \frac{E[X]}{a}\)$

Chebyshev's inequality: For any \(X\) with finite variance: $\(\Pr(|X - E[X]| \geq a) \leq \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{a^2}\)$

Chernoff / Large deviation bound: For i.i.d. \(X_1, \dots, X_t\) with mean \(c\): $\(\Pr\left(\sum_{i=1}^t X_i \leq \lambda t\right) \leq e^{-t I(\lambda)} \quad \text{if } \lambda < c\)$ where \(I(\lambda) > 0\) is the rate function.

Rate function: \(I(x) = \sup_{t} \{tx - \log E[e^{tX}]\}\) (Legendre transform of the log-MGF).

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Markov (weakest, easiest): Just need \(E[X]\). Use when \(E[X] \to 0\) and you want \(\Pr(X > 0) \to 0\).
  2. Chebyshev (medium): Need \(\text{Var}(X)\). Use when \(\text{Var}(X) \ll (E[X])^2\).
  3. Chernoff/rate function (strongest): Need MGF. Use for sums of i.i.d. variables when you need exponential tail bounds.

docs.title: Markov → First moment method
docs.text:
Statement: \(\Pr(X > 0) \leq E[X]\) for integer-valued \(X \geq 0\).

Why it matters: This IS the first moment method. If expected count goes to 0, the structure is absent w.h.p.

docs.title: Chebyshev → Second moment method
docs.text:
Statement: \(\Pr(X = 0) \leq \Pr(|X - E[X]| \geq E[X]) \leq \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{(E[X])^2}\)

Why it matters: If \(\text{Var}(X)/(E[X])^2 \to 0\), then \(X > 0\) w.h.p.

docs.title: Exponential concentration for branching processes
docs.text:
Statement: For a branching process with mean offspring \(c > 1\), conditioned on survival: $\(\Pr(c^{(1-\delta)n} \leq Z_n \leq c^{(1+\delta)n} \mid |T_X| = \infty) \to 1\)$

Upper bound proof: Markov gives \(\Pr(Z_n > c^{(1+\delta)n}) \leq c^{-\delta n}\).

Lower bound proof (the hard part): 1. Fix rate \(D = I(c^{1-\delta/2})\) from concentration inequality 2. Show that if \(Z_n\) drops below the target, there must be a "bad step" where the sum of offspring is too small 3. Union bound over bad steps, each bounded by \(e^{-Dk_s}\) 4. Sum geometric series to get overall bound \(\to 0\)

docs.title: Chebyshev for wedge count
docs.text:
Statement: \(\text{Var}(N_w) \leq C\left(\frac{1}{n^{3/2}p} + \frac{1}{n^3 p^2}\right)(E[N_w])^2\)

Why it matters: Shows \(N_w\) concentrates, which is needed to control \(C_{\text{global}} \approx p\).

Proof idea: Compute \(E[N_w^2]\) by case analysis on overlap between two wedges (0, 1, or 2 shared edges), then bound \(\text{Var} = E[N_w^2] - (E[N_w])^2\).

docs.title: Rate function bound for SIR
docs.text:
Statement: For SIR offspring with mean \(c\) and \(c \leq x \leq 1\): $\(I(x) \geq \frac{(x-c)^2}{2x(1+x)}\)$

Why it matters: Gives the exponential tail \(\Pr(|T_{\text{SIR}}| > k) \leq e^{-(1-c)^2 k/4}\) below criticality.

docs.title: Multiplicative Chernov Bound
docs.text:
Statement. Let \(S_k = \sum_{i=1}^k Y_i\) with \(Y_i\) i.i.d. and either: - exponential with mean 1, or - Poisson with arbitrary mean, or - supported on \([0,1]\).

Then for \(0 < \epsilon < 1\), $\(\Pr(S_k \geq (1+\epsilon)\mathbb{E}[S_k]) \leq e^{-\epsilon^2 \mathbb{E}[S_k]/(2(1+\epsilon))}\)$ $\(\Pr(S_k \leq (1-\epsilon)\mathbb{E}[S_k]) \leq e^{-\epsilon^2 \mathbb{E}[S_k]/2}\)$

Why it matters. Strictly stronger than additive Chernov when \(\mathbb{E}[S_k]\) is small. This is the tool that makes BFS-style giant-component arguments rigorous, because the per-step deviation is controlled relative to the current expectation.

Recognition signal. - You want a tail bound relative to \(\mathbb{E}[S_k]\), not an absolute deviation. - \(S_k\) is a sum of bounded or exponential or Poisson i.i.d. terms. - Used inside a "BFS exploration" or "branching process approximation" where you compare the running count to its mean.

Proof recipe (memorize this!). 1. Bound MGF per term: - Exp(1): \(\mathbb{E}[e^{tY}] = 1/(1-t)\) exactly. - \([0,1]\)-valued: by convexity \(\mathbb{E}[e^{tY}] \leq 1+\mathbb{E}[Y](e^t-1) \leq e^{(e^t-1)\mathbb{E}[Y]}\). 2. Markov on \(e^{tS_k}\). 3. Optimize \(t\): - Exp upper tail: \(t = \epsilon/(1+\epsilon)\). - \([0,1]\) upper tail: \(t = \log(1+\epsilon)\). - Lower tails: replace \(t \to -t\), repeat with \(\mathbb{E}[e^{-tY}]\). 4. Clean up exponent via either: - Integral form: \(\epsilon - \log(1+\epsilon) = \int_0^\epsilon \frac{x\,dx}{1+x} \geq \frac{\epsilon^2}{2(1+\epsilon)}\). - Series form: \(-\log(1-\epsilon)-\epsilon = \sum_{k\geq 2}\epsilon^k/k \geq \epsilon^2/2\). - Taylor with Lagrange remainder: for \(f(\epsilon) = (1+\epsilon)\log(1+\epsilon)-\epsilon\), \(f(0)=f'(0)=0\), \(f''(\xi)=1/(1+\xi) \geq 1/(1+\epsilon)\) for \(\xi \in [0,\epsilon]\).

Common pitfall. Skipping the optimization step. The MGF bound holds for every \(t\); it's only sharp at the optimal \(t\).

Two micro-lemmas worth remembering. - Convexity → MGF bound on [0,1]: \(f(x)=e^{tx}\) convex, so \(f(x) \leq (1-x)f(0) + xf(1)\). Take expectation. This is the entire reason the [0,1] bound exists. - \(1+x \leq e^x\): the workhorse that lets you upgrade \(\mathbb{E}[e^{tY}] \leq 1 + \mathbb{E}[Y](e^t-1)\) to the exponential form.

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Using Markov when you need Chebyshev (Markov only gives \(\Pr(X \geq a) \leq E[X]/a\), often too weak)
  • Computing variance wrong by missing correlation terms in \(E[X^2]\)
  • In the rate function: the bound \(I(x) \geq (x-c)^2/(2x(1+x))\) only holds for \(c \leq x \leq 1\)
  • Forgetting that \(I(\lambda)\) must be strictly positive — this requires \(\lambda \neq c\)
  • In the branching process concentration: mixing up the conditioning on survival with unconditional probabilities

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Markov is free, Chebyshev costs a variance, Chernoff costs an MGF"
  • "Variance of a count = sum over pairs, split by how much they overlap"
  • "\(\text{Var}(X) \ll (E[X])^2\) means \(X\) concentrates"
  • "Rate function \(I > 0\) means exponential decay — the strongest type of concentration"

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text:
The configuration model is a way to build a random graph with a prescribed degree sequence. You start with a degree sequence \((d_1, \dots, d_n)\), attach \(d_i\) "half-edges" to each vertex \(i\), then pair all half-edges uniformly at random into a perfect matching. The result is a multi-graph (can have self-loops and multi-edges) that has exactly the degree sequence you wanted.

This model is the workhorse for studying random graphs beyond \(G(n,p)\) — it lets you control the degree distribution while keeping randomness in the edge structure.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Ex. A networking event. There are n people. Person i says, "I want to end up talking to exactly \(d_i\) people". Alice wants 3 conversations, Bob 2, Carol 1, David 2

Prescribed degree sequence: (\(d_1, ..., d_n\)), specifically (3,2,1,2)

Half-edges: Each vertex \(i\) gets a set \(H_i\) of \(d_i\) half-edges. Total half-edges: \(\ell_n = \sum_i d_i\) (must be even, because every actual edge uses 2 half-edges). Analogy: conversation slots. \(H_{\text{Alice}} = \{\text{A1}, \text{A2}, \text{A3}\}\) \(H_{\text{Bob}}=\{\text{B1},\text{B2}\}\)

Random perfect matching: A partition of all half-edges into \(\ell_n/2\) disjoint pairs. Total number of perfect matchings: \((\ell_n - 1)!! = (l_n-1)(l_n-3)...3\cdot1\) all possible ways to pair everyone's conversation slots

double factorial: m!! = m(m-2)(m-4)

Adjacency matrix from matching: $\(A_{ij} = \sum_{\alpha \in H_i} \sum_{\beta \in H_j} m_{\alpha,\beta} \quad (i \neq j), \qquad A_{ii} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{\alpha,\beta \in H_i: \alpha \neq \beta} m_{\alpha,\beta}\)$

Matching indicator: \(m_{\alpha,\beta} = 1\) if half-edges \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are paired.

Key probability facts: - \(\Pr(\text{specific pair matched}) = \frac{1}{\ell_n - 1}\) - \(\Pr(\text{two disjoint pairs both matched}) = \frac{1}{(\ell_n - 1)(\ell_n - 3)}\)

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Write the quantity in terms of matching indicators \(m_{\alpha,\beta}\)
  2. Use \(E[m_{\alpha,\beta}] = 1/(\ell_n - 1)\) for single pair probabilities
  3. Use \(E[m_{\alpha,\beta} m_{\alpha',\beta'}] = 1/[(\ell_n-1)(\ell_n-3)]\) for disjoint pairs
  4. Count candidate structures (how many ways can the structure appear), then multiply by probability per structure
  5. For graph probability: Use the decentralized algorithm — each vertex independently assigns its half-edges to edges, then combine local choices into a global matching

docs.title: Lemma: Matching pair probability
docs.text:
Statement: For any fixed pair \(\{\alpha, \beta\} \subset H\), \(E[m_{\alpha,\beta}] = \frac{1}{\ell_n - 1}\).

Why it matters: This is the building block for every expected count in the configuration model.

Intuition: Half-edge \(\alpha\) must be paired with one of \(\ell_n - 1\) others, all equally likely.

Proof idea: Symmetry of the uniform random matching.

docs.title: Lemma: Disjoint pair independence
docs.text:
Statement: For disjoint pairs \(\{\alpha,\beta\}, \{\alpha',\beta'\}\): \(E[m_{\alpha,\beta} m_{\alpha',\beta'}] = \frac{1}{(\ell_n-1)(\ell_n-3)}\).

Why it matters: Needed for second moment / variance calculations (multi-edges, etc.)

Intuition: After fixing the first pair, there are \(\ell_n - 3\) remaining options for the second.

docs.title: Lemma: Number of perfect matchings
docs.text:
Statement: The number of perfect matchings of \(M\) elements is \((M-1)!!\)

Proof idea: Sequential algorithm — pick lowest element, pair it with one of \(M-1\) others, recurse. Gives \((M-1)(M-3)\cdots 1\) choices.

docs.title: Lemma: Graph probability formula
docs.text:
Statement: \(P(G) = \frac{1}{(\ell_n - 1)!!} \prod_i \frac{d_i!}{2^{A_{ii}} A_{ii}!} \prod_{i<j} \frac{1}{A_{ij}!}\)

Why it matters: Gives the exact probability of any specific multigraph in the configuration model.

Proof idea: Count \(N(G)\) = number of matchings producing \(G\), via decentralized algorithm. Each vertex independently makes local matching choices. For simple graphs: \(N(G) = \prod_i d_i!\). For general multigraphs: account for self-loop matchings \(((2A_{ii}-1)!!)\) and multi-edge overcounting \((A_{ij}!)\).

docs.title: Lemma: Giant component size via ODE
docs.text:
Statement: For \(p = c/n\) with \(c > 1\), the giant component has size \(\approx n\theta\) where \(\theta\) solves \(\theta + e^{-c\theta} = 1\) (same as survival probability of \(\text{Poi}(c)\) branching process).

Proof idea: 1. Track BFS queue \(\tilde{Y}_t\) and undiscovered vertices \(N_t\) 2. Rescale: \(S(\tau) = \frac{1}{n}E[N_{\lfloor \tau n \rfloor}]\), \(I(\tau) = \frac{1}{n}E[\tilde{Y}_{\lfloor \tau n \rfloor}]\) 3. Get ODEs: \(S' = -cS\), \(I' = cS - 1\) 4. Solve: \(S(\tau) = e^{-c\tau}\), \(I(\tau) = 1 - e^{-c\tau} - \tau\) 5. Component size = where \(I(\theta) = 0\), i.e., \(\theta + e^{-c\theta} = 1\)

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting the factor of \(1/2\) in self-loop count (matching \(\{\alpha,\beta\}\) is unordered)
  • Using \((\ell_n - 1)^2\) instead of \((\ell_n - 1)(\ell_n - 3)\) for two disjoint pairs — the second pair has fewer options
  • Confusing \(A_{ij}\) (number of edges between \(i,j\)) with the indicator \(1[A_{ij} \geq 1]\)
  • In the ODE method: forgetting that \(\tilde{Y}_t\) can go negative (unlike the stopped process \(Y_t\)), but both give the same stopping time

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Half-edges are the atoms, matching is the randomness"
  • "\(1/(\ell_n - 1)\) is like \(1/(2m-1)\) — the chance your half-edge finds a specific partner"
  • "Decentralized algorithm = each vertex makes local choices independently, then combine"
  • "ODE method: zoom out by factor \(n\), discrete steps become derivatives"

docs.title: Chung-Lu model [2002]
docs.text:
Fix weights \(w_1, \ldots, w_n \ge 0\) with \(\ell_n = \sum_i w_i\). Connect \(i \ne j\) independently with prob \(p_{ij} = w_i w_j / \ell_n\) (and \(p_{ii} = w_i^2 / (2\ell_n)\)). Assumption: \(\max_i w_i^2 / \ell_n \le 1\) ensures probabilities \(\le 1\).

Expected degree: \(E[d_i] = w_i\). Limit law: \(d_i \to \text{Poi}(w_i)\) if \(\max_i w_i^2/\ell_n \to 0\). Theorem (under suitable conditions on \(w\)): 1. Giant component exists iff \(\tilde c := \sum w_i^2 / \sum w_i > 1\). 2. \(\text{diam}(G) \approx \log_{\tilde c} n\).

docs.title: Molloy-Reed model [1995]
docs.text: \(d = (d_1, \ldots, d_n)\) degree sequence. \(G \sim UG_n(d)\) uniform over simple graphs with degree sequence \(d\). Existence: \(d\) is graphic iff there exists a simple graph with those degrees (\(\sum d_i\) even, etc. — Erdős–Gallai conditions).

docs.title: Erdős–Gallai (Havel-Hakimi) algorithm
docs.text: Greedy: while \(\sum d_i > 0\): order degrees decreasing, attach \(d_1\) edges from vertex 1 to next \(d_1\) vertices, decrement, repeat. Fails iff \(d\) not graphic; otherwise outputs a graph with degrees \(d\).

docs.title: SWAP / FLIP algorithm (sample uniformly from \(UG_n(d)\))
docs.text:
• Start with one legal graph.
  • Pick edges \(\{i,j\}, \{u,v\}\) uniformly. Delete and rematch the four endpoints randomly into two new edges.
  • Repeat \(T\) times.

Theorem (Cooper-Dyer-Greenhill '07). For \(d\)-regular graphs, SWAP mixes in poly time in \(n, d, \log(1/\epsilon)\). Theorem (Feder-Guetz-Mihail-Saberi '06, FLIP variant). If \(\max d_i \le \sqrt n\), FLIP mixes in \(O(n^{4d}(\log n + \log(1/\epsilon)))\).

docs.title: Simplicity probability for \(\text{CM}_n(d)\)
docs.text:
Define \( u = E[D(D-1)]/E[D]\) (expected excess degree). Theorem. Under \(D \sim p_k\) with \(E[D^2] < \infty\): - \(\# ext{multi-edges} o ext{Poi}( u^2/4)\) - \(\# ext{loops} o ext{Poi}( u/2)\) - \(\Pr( ext{simple}) o e^{- u/2 - u^2/4}\)

Corollary. \(\Pr(G ext{ simple}) o\) constant \(> 0\), so conditioning on simplicity (rejection sampling) gives a uniform sample from simple graphs with that degree sequence in \(O(1)\) expected trials.

Heuristic (loops). $\Pr( ext{half-edge \(\alpha\) at \(i\) pairs with another half-edge at \(i\)}) = \prod_{k=1}^{d_i-1}(1 - (d_i-k)/\ell_n) \approx e^{-d_i(d_i-1)/(2\ell_n)}$. So \(\Pr( ext{no loop at }i) \approx e^{-d_i(d_i-1)/(2\ell_n)}\), multiplying gives \(\Pr( ext{no loops total}) \approx e^{- u/2}\).

docs.title: Recognition signals
docs.text:
• "Sample uniformly from graphs with degree sequence \(d\)" → Molloy-Reed via CM + rejection.
  • "Power-law degree distribution with weights \(w_i\)" → Chung-Lu.
  • "\(\Pr(\text{simple})\) stays bounded away from 0" → use \(e^{-\nu/2 - \nu^2/4}\).

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: A threshold is a critical value of a parameter (usually \(p\) in \(G(n,p)\) or \(c\) in \(p = c/n\)) where a graph property flips from "almost surely absent" to "almost surely present." The proof strategy is almost always: compute the expected count of some structure, then show it goes to 0 below the threshold (first moment method) and to infinity above it (second moment method or branching process argument).

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Threshold for property \(P\): A function \(p^*(n)\) such that: - If \(p \gg p^*\), then \(\Pr(G(n,p) \text{ has } P) \to 1\) - If \(p \ll p^*\), then \(\Pr(G(n,p) \text{ has } P) \to 0\)

First moment method (upper bound on existence): $\(\Pr(X > 0) \leq E[X]\)$ Or it can also be $\(Pr(X \ge 1) \le E[X]\)$ If \(E[X] \to 0\), then the structure is absent w.h.p. X is the number of isolated vertices here.

Second moment method (lower bound on existence): $\(\Pr(X > 0) \geq \frac{(E[X])^2}{E[X^2]}\)$ If this ratio \(\to 1\), the structure exists w.h.p.

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Define an indicator-based count \(X = \sum I_i\) for the structure of interest
  2. First moment (easy direction): Compute \(E[X]\), show it \(\to 0\) on one side
  3. Second moment (hard direction): Compute \(E[X^2]\), show \(E[X^2]/(E[X])^2 \to 1\) on the other side
  4. The second moment calculation always involves splitting \(E[X^2]\) into diagonal (\(i=j\)) and off-diagonal (\(i \neq j\)) terms and bounding the correlation

docs.title: Lemma: Threshold for isolated vertices in \(G(n,p)\)
docs.text:
Statement: The threshold for isolated vertices is \(p^* = \frac{\log n}{n}\). - If \(p \geq c\frac{\log n}{n}\) with \(c > 1\): no isolated vertices w.h.p. - If \(p \leq c\frac{\log n}{n}\) with \(c < 1\): at least one isolated vertex w.h.p.

Proof (absence above threshold — first moment): - \(X = \sum I_i\) where \(I_i = 1\) if vertex \(i\) is isolated - \(E[X] = n(1-p)^{n-1} \leq ne^{-p(n-1)} \leq n^{1-c} e^{c\log n/n} \to 0\) when \(c > 1\) - useful bound: \(e^{-p}\ge 1-p \qquad \text{for all } p\in\mathbb R.\)

Proof (existence below threshold — second moment): - \(E[X^2] = n(1-p)^{n-1} + n(n-1)(1-p)^{2n-3}\) - Note the exponent \(2n-3\) (not \(2(n-1)\)) because vertices \(i,j\) share the edge \(\{i,j\}\) - Ratio: \(\frac{E[X^2]}{(E[X])^2} = \frac{1}{n(1-p)^{n-1}} + \frac{1}{1-p} \to 1\) when \(c < 1\)

docs.title: Lemma: No cycles when \(p \ll 1/n\)
docs.text:
Statement: If \(p \ll 1/n\), then \(G(n,p)\) contains no cycles w.h.p., and all components are trees of size \(o(\log n)\).

Proof idea: 1. Let \(Y=\sum_{k\le3}\) where \(Y_k\) is the number of cycles of length k in G(n, p) 2. Count the ways for each k, there are \(\frac{k!}{2k}\) ways to form the cycle 3. Find \(E[Y_k]\) (Let indicator \(I_c = 1\) if there is a cycle) = \(\binom{n}{k}\frac{k!}{2k}p^k\) 4. Given that the formula is for "from n choose k" regardless of sequence$\(\binom{n}{k}=\frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}=\frac{1}{k!}\prod_{i=1}^{k-1}(n-i)\le\frac{n^k}{k!}\)$ - Expected cycles of length \(k\): \(E[Y_k] \leq (np)^k\) 5. Now we use first moment method because we are trying to conclude that w.h.p. \(G(n, p)\) contains no cycles. Given \(np<1\), \(E[Y] \le \frac{(np)^3}{1-pn}\) 6. Total: \(E[Y] \leq \sum_{k \geq 3} (np)^k = \frac{(np)^3}{1-np} \to 0\) if \(p\ll \frac{1}{n}\) - Then we need to prove the tree bound. From part 1, we conclude that in this range, all connected components of \(G(n,p)\) are trees. Denote the number of trees of size k be \(X_k\), - Tree size bound via \(E[X_k] \leq \frac{1}{epk^2}(npe)^k\) and Markov on \(X_{\geq k}\)

docs.title: Key approximation
docs.text: \((1-p)^n \approx e^{-pn}\) — used constantly. More precisely: \(\log(1-p) \geq -p - p^2\) for \(p \leq 1/2\).

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Using \((1-p)^{2(n-1)}\) instead of \((1-p)^{2n-3}\) for two isolated vertices — forgetting the shared edge between \(i\) and \(j\)
  • In second moment: forgetting that \(E[I_i I_j] \neq E[I_i]E[I_j]\) (indicators are correlated through shared edges/vertices)
  • Bounding \((1-p)^n\) by \(e^{-pn}\) when you need a lower bound — this only works as an upper bound. For lower bounds use \(\log(1-p) \geq -p - p^2\)
  • Forgetting the \(1/2\) for unoriented cycles (counting both directions)
  • In tree counting: the \(k^{k-2}\) formula is for labeled trees, not unlabeled

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "First moment kills it (structure gone), second moment saves it (structure exists)"
  • "\(E[X^2]/(E[X])^2\): the closer to 1, the more concentrated \(X\) is"
  • "The shared edge between \(i\) and \(j\) is always where the correlation lives"
  • "Threshold = the \(p\) where the expected count crosses from \(\to 0\) to \(\to \infty\)"

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: In \(G(n, p)\) with \(p = c/n\), when \(c > 1\) a unique component of size \(\Theta(n)\) emerges — the giant. Its asymptotic size fraction \(\theta\) is the survival probability of a Poisson(\(c\)) branching process. The standard proof tracks BFS exploration as a stochastic process and shows it concentrates around a deterministic fluid limit \(f(x) = 1 - e^{-cx} - x\).

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
• \(G(n, p)\): each pair of vertices is an edge independently with probability \(p\).
  • Sparse regime: \(p = c/n\) for fixed \(c > 0\).
  • BFS exploration of \(C(x)\): maintain Removed / Active / Unexplored. At step \(t+1\), pop one active vertex, reveal its neighbors among unexplored.
  • Active queue size: \(\tilde Y_t\) with \(\tilde Y_0 = 1\), \(\tilde Y_{t+1} = \tilde Y_t + X_{t+1} - 1\).
  • Step revelation: \(X_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t \sim \text{Bin}(N_t, c/n)\), where \(N_t\) = number of unexplored.
  • Component size: \(|C(x)| = \min\{t : \tilde Y_t = 0\} = \min\{t : \tilde Y_t \leq 0\}\).
  • Giant fraction \(\theta\): non-trivial solution of \(\theta + e^{-c\theta} = 1\), equivalently the unique \(\theta \in (0,1)\) with \(f(\theta) = 0\) where \(f(x) = 1 - e^{-cx} - x\).

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Set up BFS exploration, write the \(\tilde Y_t\) recurrence.
  2. Find the binomial: show \(N_t \sim \text{Bin}(n-1, (1-c/n)^t)\) via the "each unexplored vertex independently survives each round" coupling. Then \(\Delta_t := n-1-N_t \sim \text{Bin}(n-1, 1 - (1-c/n)^t)\) and \(\tilde Y_t = \Delta_t + 1 - t\).
  3. Apply multiplicative Chernov to \(\Delta_t\) (NOT additive — need relative bound, since \(\mathbb{E}[\Delta_t]\) varies in \(t\)).
  4. Sandwich with \(f_{\pm\epsilon}\): \(f_{-\epsilon}(x) = (1-\epsilon)(1-e^{-cx}) - x \leq \tilde Y_t/n \leq f_{+\epsilon}(x) + O(1/n) = (1+\epsilon)(1-e^{-cx}) - x + O(1/n)\) w.h.p.
  5. Analyze zeros of \(f_{\pm\epsilon}\): each is strictly convex, has exactly one nontrivial zero \(x_\pm(\epsilon)\), and \(x_\pm(\epsilon) \to \theta\) as \(\epsilon \to 0\).
  6. Convert to bounds on \(|C(x)|\): lower from sandwich ⇒ \(\tilde Y_t > 0\) until \(t \approx (\theta-\delta)n\); upper from sandwich ⇒ \(\tilde Y_t < 0\) by \(t \approx (\theta+\delta)n\).
  7. Union bound over \(t \in [K\log n, n]\): the geometric tail of \(e^{-\epsilon^2 t / 8}\) gives \(O(e^{-\epsilon^2 K \log n / 8}) \to 0\), hence "w.h.p. for all \(t\) in range".

docs.title: Lemma: BFS active queue is binomially distributed
docs.text:
Statement. \(N_t \sim \text{Bin}(n-1, (1-c/n)^t)\), so \(\Delta_t = X_1 + \dots + X_t \sim \text{Bin}(n-1, 1 - (1-c/n)^t)\), and $\(\tilde Y_t = \Delta_t + 1 - t.\)$

Why it matters. The whole concentration argument runs through this. Without a binomial form, you can't apply Chernov.

Intuition. Each unexplored vertex independently survives each round with prob \(1-c/n\).

Proof idea. By induction on \(t\). Conditional on \(N_{t-1}\), \(X_t \sim \text{Bin}(N_{t-1}, c/n)\) so \(N_t = N_{t-1} - X_t \sim \text{Bin}(N_{t-1}, 1-c/n)\). Thinning of binomial = binomial with multiplied parameter.

Recognition signal. Anytime BFS or local exploration is happening on \(G(n,p)\), immediately convert to "each unexplored vertex independently survives".

Common pitfall. Forgetting that \(N_t\) is binomial (not just "approximately Poisson") — you need the exact binomial form to apply multiplicative Chernov.

docs.title: Lemma: Fluid-limit sandwich
docs.text:
Statement. For \(0 < \epsilon < 1\) and \(n\) large, with prob \(\geq 1 - O(e^{-\epsilon^2 t / 8})\), $\(f_{-\epsilon}(t/n) \leq \tilde Y_t / n \leq f_{+\epsilon}(t/n) + O(1/n).\)$

Why it matters. Converts "stochastic process near its mean" into "process near a deterministic fluid limit." This is what lets you analyze zeros of \(f\) instead of zeros of the random process.

Proof idea. Multiplicative Chernov on \(\Delta_t\) gives \(|\Delta_t - \mathbb{E}[\Delta_t]| \leq \epsilon \mathbb{E}[\Delta_t]\), then \(\mathbb{E}[\Delta_t]/n \to 1 - e^{-cx}\) by \(1 - (1 - c/n)^t = 1 - e^{-ct/n + O(t/n^2)}\).

Recognition signal. Any "the random process tracks a deterministic curve w.h.p." statement.

docs.title: Lemma: \(\theta\) is the BP survival probability
docs.text:
Statement. The non-trivial zero \(\theta\) of \(f(x) = 1 - e^{-cx} - x\) satisfies \(\theta + e^{-c\theta} = 1\), which is the same equation as the survival probability of a Poisson(\(c\)) branching process: \(1-\theta = e^{-c\theta}\) (since \(1-\theta\) is the extinction probability and equals the PGF of \(\text{Poi}(c)\) at \(1-\theta\)).

Why it matters. Connects giant component to branching process — the foundational "BFS ≈ BP early on" picture.

Intuition. Early in BFS exploration, the unexplored pool is essentially "infinite" (depleted by \(O(1/n)\)), so each new vertex has \(\approx \text{Bin}(n, c/n) \approx \text{Poi}(c)\) children. So local BFS = \(\text{Poi}(c)\) branching tree. The component survives ↔ BP survives ↔ probability \(\theta\).

docs.title: Lemma: Geometry of \(f_{\pm\epsilon}\)
docs.text:
Statement. If \(\epsilon < 1 - 1/c\), then \(f_{-\epsilon}(x) = (1-\epsilon)(1-e^{-cx}) - x\) has exactly two zeros in \([0,1]\): \(x = 0\) and \(x_-(\epsilon) \in (0, 1)\). Moreover \(x_-(\epsilon) \leq \theta\) and \(x_-(\epsilon) \to \theta\) as \(\epsilon \to 0\). Symmetrically for \(f_{+\epsilon}\).

Why it matters. Tells you exactly where the sandwich pinches the random process.

Proof idea. \(f_{-\epsilon}'(0) = (1-\epsilon)c - 1 > 0\) when \(\epsilon < 1 - 1/c\), \(f_{-\epsilon}(1) < 0\), \(f_{-\epsilon}\) is strictly convex ⇒ exactly one nontrivial zero. Convergence \(x_-(\epsilon) \to \theta\) uses \(f\) is strictly convex with \(f(\theta)=0\), so \(f(\theta - \delta) > 0\), and \(f_{-\epsilon}(\theta - \delta) \to f(\theta - \delta) > 0\) by continuity in \(\epsilon\).

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Using additive Chernov when multiplicative is required.
  • Forgetting \(|C(x)| \geq K\log n\) conditioning — small components are dominated by branching-process noise, not the fluid limit.
  • Treating the trivial zero \(x=0\) as the answer for component size — always go for the non-trivial zero \(\theta\).
  • Forgetting that \(\theta > 0\) requires \(c > 1\) (else \(f'(0) = c - 1 \leq 0\) and the only root is \(x=0\)).
  • Confusing "fraction of unexplored remaining" \((1-c/n)^t\) with "Poisson(\(ct/n\)) probability of being missed". They agree to leading order but the binomial is the rigorous form.

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "BFS active queue does a Poisson(\(c\)) branching process — until the unexplored pool runs out." The \(-x\) term is the depletion correction.
  • \(f(x) = 1 - e^{-cx} - x\): "fraction explored minus fraction popped."
  • "\(\theta\) is where the queue would deterministically die." Past \(\theta n\), the depletion overwhelms the offspring rate.
  • Sandwich plot: draw \(f\) as a strictly convex hill that starts at 0, rises to a peak, then dips back below 0 at \(\theta\). \(f_{\pm\epsilon}\) are slightly squashed/stretched copies that pinch \(\theta\) from both sides as \(\epsilon \to 0\).

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: A branching process models a population where each individual independently produces a random number of offspring. The key question is: does the population survive forever or die out? The answer depends on the mean offspring \(c = E[X]\): if \(c \leq 1\), the process dies out almost surely; if \(c > 1\), there's a positive probability of survival. Branching processes appear everywhere in this course as approximations to early-stage epidemic spread, BFS exploration of random graphs, and component growth.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Branching process \(T_X\): Start with one individual. Each individual independently has \(X\) offspring (drawn from distribution \(D_X\)).
