Generation sizes: \(Z_0 = 1\), \(Z_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{Z_n} X_i\) where \(X_i\) are i.i.d. copies of \(X\).

Mean offspring: \(c = E[X]\). We have \(E[Z_n] = c^n\).

Probability generating function (PGF): \(G(\lambda) = E[\lambda^X] = \sum_k p_k \lambda^k\).

Extinction probability: \(\eta = \Pr(|T_X| < \infty)\) is the smallest non-negative solution of \(G(\eta) = \eta\).

Survival probability: \(\theta = 1 - \eta = \Pr(|T_X| = \infty)\).

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Compute mean \(c = E[X]\) — this tells you if process is sub/supercritical
  2. Write PGF \(G(\lambda) = E[\lambda^X]\)
  3. Solve \(G(\eta) = \eta\) for extinction probability (take smallest solution in \([0,1]\))
  4. For growth rate: \(E[Z_n] = c^n\), and conditioned on survival, \(\frac{1}{n}\log Z_n \to \log c\)

docs.title: Lemma: Extinction criterion
docs.text:
Statement: \(\theta > 0\) iff \(c > 1\).

Intuition: If each individual produces on average more than one offspring, the population can sustain itself.

docs.title: Lemma: Exponential growth
docs.text:
Statement: Conditioned on survival, \(Z_n\) grows exponentially: $\(\Pr(c^{(1-\delta)n} \leq Z_n \leq c^{(1+\delta)n} \mid |T_X| = \infty) \to 1\)$

Proof outline: - Upper bound: Markov: \(\Pr(Z_n > c^{(1+\delta)n}) \leq c^{-\delta n}\) - Lower bound (the hard part): 1. Apply concentration bound: \(\Pr(Z_{n+1} \leq c' t \mid Z_n = t) \leq e^{-Dt}\) for \(D = I(c')\) 2. Define increasing targets \(k_t = \lceil c^{(1-\delta/2)t} k_0 \rceil\) 3. If \(Z\) ever drops below target, there's a first "bad step" \(s\) where \(Z_{n_0+s} \geq k_s\) but \(Z_{n_0+s+1} < k_{s+1}\) 4. Union bound: \(\sum_{s} e^{-Dk_s} \leq \frac{e^{-Dk_0}}{1-e^{-D}}\) (geometric series since \(k_s \geq k_0 + s\)) 5. Use the bonus result to handle \(Z_{n_0} < k_0\)

docs.title: Lemma: Linear growth conditioned on survival
docs.text:
Statement: \(\Pr(Z_n \leq \frac{c-1}{2}n \mid |T_X| = \infty) \leq \frac{e^{-D'n}}{1-e^{-D'}}\) where \(D' = I(\frac{c+1}{2})\).

Proof idea: If \(Z_n = Y_{t_n}\) (BFS queue at generation boundary), then \(Z_n\) small means \(Y_t\) is small for some \(t \geq n\). Apply concentration + union bound over \(t \geq n\).

docs.title: PGF for Poisson offspring
docs.text:
Statement: If \(X \sim \text{Poi}(c)\), then \(G(\lambda) = e^{-c(1-\lambda)}\).

Extinction equation: \(\eta = e^{-c(1-\eta)}\), equivalently \(\theta + e^{-c\theta} = 1\) (same as giant component equation!).

docs.title: PGF for SIR offspring
docs.text:
Statement: \(G_c(\lambda) = \frac{1}{1+c(1-\lambda)}\) (limiting form).

Extinction equation: \(\eta = 1/c\), so \(P = 1 - 1/c\).

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Confusing \(\eta\) (extinction) with \(\theta\) (survival): \(\theta = 1 - \eta\)
  • Solving \(G(\eta) = \eta\) but taking the wrong root — always take the smallest in \([0,1]\)
  • Forgetting that exponential growth is conditional on survival — unconditionally, \(Z_n\) could be 0
  • In the lower bound proof: trying to apply concentration directly to \(Z_n\) instead of going through the step-by-step argument with targets \(k_t\)
  • Mixing up \((1-\delta)\) vs \((1-\delta/2)\) in the growth rate — the \(\delta/2\) slack is needed for the inductive argument

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "\(c > 1\) = supercritical = survives; \(c \leq 1\) = dies"
  • "\(E[Z_n] = c^n\) is easy. Showing \(Z_n \approx c^n\) w.h.p. is the real work"
  • "Upper bound is Markov (one line). Lower bound is the union-bound-over-targets argument"
  • "PGF fixed point = extinction probability"

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: For \(G(n, c/n)\) with \(c > 1\), the diameter of the giant component (typical pairwise distance) is \(\Theta(\log n)\), with sharp constant \(1/\log c\). The proof has two halves: (a) lower bound via expected count of short paths, (b) upper bound via BFS exploration showing the queue grows like \(c^t\), then a "sprinkling" / connection step where two BFS trees from \(x\) and \(y\) meet.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
• Diameter (course convention): typical distance \(D = \text{dist}_{G(n,p)}(x,y)\) for \(x, y\) uniform from \(C_{\max}\).
  • BFS queue at generation \(a\): \(Z_a = Y_{t(a)}\) where \(t(a)\) is the time the last vertex of generation \(a-1\) leaves the queue.
  • Generation recurrence: \(t(a+1) = t(a) + Z_a\), with \(t(1) = 1\), \(Z_1 = X_1 = Y_1\).
  • Heuristic: \(Y_t \approx (c-1)t\), so \(Z_a \approx (c-1) c^{a-1}\), \(t(a) \approx c^{a-1}\).

docs.title: 3. Main theorem
docs.text: Theorem. For \(c > 1\) in \(G(n, c/n)\): $\(\frac{D}{\log_c n} \to_P 1.\)$

docs.title: 4. Proof structure
docs.text: Need to show for all \(\epsilon > 0\): - (a) \(\Pr(D \le (1-\epsilon) \log_c n) \to 0\) — diameter not too short. - (b) \(\Pr(D \ge (1+\epsilon) \log_c n) \to 0\) — diameter not too long.

docs.title: (a) Lower bound — expected path count
docs.text:
Claim: For any \(x \ne y\) and \(\ell_0 = (1-\epsilon)\log_c n\): $\(\Pr_{G(n,p)}(d(x,y) \le \ell_0) \le \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{c^{\ell_0}}{1 - 1/c} = \frac{n^{-\epsilon}}{1 - 1/c}.\)$

Proof. Let \(X_\ell = \#\text{paths } x \to y \text{ of length } \ell\). Then \(E[X_\ell] = (\#\text{paths}) \cdot p^\ell \le n^{\ell-1} p^\ell = c^\ell / n\). Sum geometric series.

Conclusion. Compute: $\(\Pr(D \le \ell_0) \le \Pr(D \le \ell_0, |C_{\max}| \ge \theta n / 2) + \Pr(|C_{\max}| < \theta n / 2)\)$ where the first term is bounded by \((4/(\theta^2 n^2)) \sum_{x,y} \Pr(d(x,y) \le \ell_0) \le O(1/n) + O(n^{-\epsilon}) \to 0\).

docs.title: (b) Upper bound — BFS exploration meets at log
docs.text:
Step 1. Bounds on \(t(a)\) and \(Y_t\) from concentration: - Claim 1: Set \(a_+ = (1 - 2\delta)\log_c n\). Then \(\Pr(t(a_+) \ge n^{1-\delta}) = O(n^{-\delta})\). (Markov on \(E[t(a)] \le c^a/(c-1)\).) - Claim 2: Set \(t_- = \delta \log_c n\), \(t_+ = n^{1-\delta}\). Then \(\Pr(|C(x)| \ge t_+ \text{ and } \exists t \in [t_-, t_+]: Y_t \le (c-1-\delta)t) = O(n^{-\alpha})\). (Concentration via SIR-type rate function on offspring sums.)

Step 2. Assume both events: \(t(a_+) \le t_+\) and \(Y_t \ge (c-1-\delta)t\) for all \(t \in [t_-, t_+]\). Then iterating \(t(a+1) = t(a) + Y_{t(a)} \ge t(a) + (c-1-\delta)t(a) = (c-\delta)t(a)\) gives $\(t(a) \ge (c-\delta)^{a - a_-} t(a_-) \quad \text{for } a \in [a_-, a_+].\)$

Step 3 (key claim). Set \(a_n = (\tfrac12 + \tfrac\epsilon3 + \delta)\log_c n\). Then for \(\delta\) small: $\(Z_{a_n} \ge n^{1/2 + \epsilon/4}.\)$

Step 4 (meeting point). Run BFS from both \(x\) and \(y\) to depth \(a_n\). Both have \(\ge n^{1/2 + \epsilon/4}\) vertices in the frontier. Probability no edge between the two frontiers is \(\le (1-p)^{(n^{1/2+\epsilon/4})^2} \le e^{-p \cdot n^{1+\epsilon/2}} \to 0\).

Step 5. Combined diameter \(\le 1 + 2 a_n = 1 + 2(\tfrac12 + \tfrac\epsilon3 + \delta)\log_c n \le (1+\epsilon)\log_c n\) for \(\delta\) small enough.

docs.title: 5. Method extraction
docs.text:
• Method: Generation recurrence \(t(a+1) = t(a) + Z_a\) + concentration on \(Y_t\) + "sprinkling" / two-BFS meeting argument.
  • Recognition signal: "diameter / typical distance in giant" → log scale, constant \(1/\log c\). Same shape applies to CM and PA.
  • First move: lower bound by counting paths (geometric series in \(c\)); upper bound by BFS exploration to depth \(\sim \frac12 \log_c n\) from both endpoints.
  • Common mistake:
  • Trying to bound \(D\) via single BFS to depth \(\log_c n\) — that's the whole diameter, but the meeting argument cuts depth in half (each side \(\frac12 \log_c n\)).
  • Forgetting \(|C_{\max}| \ge \theta n\) conditioning when picking uniform pair.
  • Reusable lesson: "Two BFS trees of size \(\sqrt n\) meet w.h.p." is the standard \(\frac12\) trick. It's what makes the constant \(1/\log c\), not \(1/\log c \cdot 2\).

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Diameter \(= \log_c n\)" — match generation count with population fraction.
  • "Each BFS tree fills to \(\sqrt n\), then they meet by birthday paradox."
  • "Generation recurrence \(t(a) \approx c^{a-1}\) — exponential growth at rate \(c\)."
  • For CM and PA the same \(\log n\) scaling holds; constant changes (use \(\nu\) instead of \(c\) for CM).

docs.title: 7. Where else it appears
docs.text: • Lecture 14 (Small World): Watts-Strogatz / Newman-Watts beat the \(\log n\) scaling at constant clustering. Standard graph models still have \(\log n\).

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: Real networks (web links, citations, retweets) are directed. The component structure is more subtle than the undirected case: instead of one giant connected component, you get a bow-tie: a central giant strongly-connected component (SCC), an in-fan that can reach the SCC, and an out-fan that the SCC can reach. The relevant model is \(D(n, p)\) (random directed Erdős–Rényi), and the analysis reduces to two coupled BFS-trees: forward (out-component) and backward (in-component).

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
• Digraph \(D = (V, E)\): each ordered pair \((i, j)\) may or may not be an edge; cycles allowed.
  • DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph): digraph with no directed cycles.
  • Strongly connected component (SCC): maximal \(C \subseteq V\) such that for all \(x, y \in C\), both \(\exists\) paths \(x \to y\) and \(y \to x\) in \(C\).
  • Out-component \(C_+(x) = \{y : \exists \text{ directed path } x \to y\}\).
  • In-component \(C_-(x) = \{y : \exists \text{ directed path } y \to x\}\).

Lemma (DAG of SCCs). Contracting each SCC to a single vertex gives a DAG \(D'\). (Any two SCCs with paths in both directions would merge into a single SCC.)

docs.title: \(D(n, p)\) — random digraph (Karp '90)
docs.text: Each of the \(n(n-1)\) ordered pairs is an edge i.i.d. with prob \(p\). Adjacency matrix entries i.i.d. \(\text{Be}(p)\).

docs.title: Oriented Configuration Model \(\text{OCM}(d^+, d^-)\) (Cooper-Frieze '02)
docs.text: Each vertex \(i\) has \(d_i^+\) out-stubs and \(d_i^-\) in-stubs. Match out-stubs to in-stubs uniformly. Constraint: \(\ell_{\text{out}} = \sum d_i^+ = \sum d_i^- = \ell_{\text{in}}\).

docs.title: Oriented Preferential Attachment OPA (Bollobas-Borgs-Chayes-Riordan '03)
docs.text:
Parameters \(\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1\), "shifts" \(\delta_{\text{in}}, \delta_{\text{out}}\). At each time step: - \(\alpha\): new vertex creates link to old \(w\) with prob \(\propto (\delta_{\text{in}} + d_w^{\text{in}})\). - \(\gamma\): old vertex \(u\) chosen with prob \(\propto (\delta_{\text{out}} + d_u^{\text{out}})\) creates link to new vertex. - \(\beta\): link \(u \to w\) added with prob \(\propto (d_u^{\text{out}} + \delta_{\text{out}})(d_w^{\text{in}} + \delta_{\text{in}})\).

Properties: OPA produces power laws for both in and out degrees, with correlated joint distribution.

docs.title: 4. Bow-tie structure for \(D(n, c/n)\), \(c > 1\)
docs.text:
Theorem. \(D(n, c/n)\) has a unique giant SCC of size \(\theta^2 n\) (where \(\theta\) = survival probability of \(\text{Poi}(c)\) BP), with: - SCC core: \(\theta^2 n\) - In-fan only (can reach SCC, not reachable from it): \(\theta(1-\theta) n\) - Out-fan only (reachable from SCC, can't reach it): \(\theta(1-\theta) n\) - Tubes / tendrils / disconnected: \((1-\theta)^2 n\) + small components \(O(\log n)\).

Picture: 

   in-fan        SCC core        out-fan
   θ(1-θ)n       θ²n             θ(1-θ)n
      \         /    \         /
       ──────●──●──●──●──────

docs.title: BFS-out tree \(T_+(x)\) has same distribution as undirected BFS
docs.text: Each vertex has \(X_t \sim \text{Bin}(n - 1, p)\) out-neighbors; the BFS-out tree has the same distribution as the BFS tree for an undirected component in \(G(n, p)\) at the same \(p\). So $\(\Pr_{D(n,p)}(|C_+(x)| \ge a) = \Pr_{G(n,p)}(|C(x)| \ge a).\)$

docs.title: Lemma 1 (dichotomy)
docs.text:
W.h.p. for all \(x\): either - Small fan-out: \(|C_+(x)| \le \frac{3c}{(c-1)^2}\log n\), OR - Large fan-out: \(|C_+(x)| \ge (\theta - \epsilon) n\).

Same for \(C_-(x)\) (replace edges by reverse edges in the BFS).

This dichotomy comes directly from the giant-component argument applied separately to forward and backward exploration.

docs.title: Define the "large" sets
docs.text: $\(L_+ = \{x : |C_+(x)| \ge \epsilon n\}, \quad L_- = \{x : |C_-(x)| \ge \epsilon n\}.\)$ \(E[|L_\pm|] \approx \theta n\) (matching the \(G(n,p)\) giant result).

docs.title: Lemma 3 (asymptotic independence of \(C_+, C_-\))
docs.text:
For events \(A, B\) on \(C_+(x), C_-(x)\) respectively: $\(\Pr(A \cap B) = \Pr(A) \Pr(B)(1 + o(1)).\)$

Why. BFS-out from \(x\) explores forward edges; BFS-in explores backward edges. Disjoint sets of edges → asymptotic independence (when not too many vertices are revealed).

docs.title: Putting it together
docs.text:
• \(L_+ \cap L_-\) = vertices with both large fan-in and fan-out → all reach each other → strongly connected.
  • \(|L_+ \cap L_-| = \sum_x \Pr(|C_+(x)|, |C_-(x)| \text{ both large}) = \sum_x \theta \cdot \theta (1+o(1)) = \theta^2 n (1+o(1))\) by independence.
  • \(|L_- \setminus L_+|\) = in-fan only \(\approx \theta(1-\theta)n\).
  • \(|L_+ \setminus L_-|\) = out-fan only \(\approx \theta(1-\theta)n\).

docs.title: 6. Method extraction
docs.text:
• Method: Coupled forward/backward BFS + asymptotic independence between in- and out-components.
  • Recognition signal: "directed graph", "SCC", "bow-tie", "OCM", "OPA".
  • First move: identify \(C_+(x), C_-(x)\), apply giant component result to each separately, then multiply by independence.
  • Common mistake:
  • Treating SCC like an undirected component — it requires both directions of reachability, hence \(\theta^2\) not \(\theta\).
  • Forgetting the asymptotic independence — without Lemma 3, you'd get \(|L_+ \cap L_-| \le \min(|L_+|, |L_-|) = \theta n\), which overcounts.
  • Reusable lesson: Bow-tie sizes follow \((\theta(1-\theta), \theta^2, \theta(1-\theta), (1-\theta)^2)\) — exactly the joint distribution of two independent \(\text{Be}(\theta)\) variables.

docs.title: 7. Connection to SIR
docs.text:
The SIR digraph is exactly a directed graph in disguise: - Forward BP = exploration of \(C_+(x)\) (who gets infected starting from \(x\)) → outbreak probability \(P_+\). - Backward BP = exploration of \(C_-(x)\) (who could have infected \(x\)) → susceptibility probability \(P_-\).

Different probabilities \(P_+ \ne P_-\) for SIR (because forward uses random \(T\) at parent, backward absorbs \(T\) into \(\bar p\)), but same threshold \(R_0 > 1\). Same algebra as the bow-tie picture here.

docs.title: 8. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Bow-tie: \(\theta(1-\theta), \theta^2, \theta(1-\theta), (1-\theta)^2\) — the four corners are products of \(\theta, 1-\theta\)."
  • "SCC = \(\theta^2\) because you need both forward AND backward survival."
  • "Forward and backward BFS are asymptotically independent — that's why it's a product."
  • "OPA: power laws on in AND out degrees, correlated."

docs.title: Stochastic Block Model and Multitype Branching Process
Stochastic Block Model and Multitype Branching Process
SBM definition
docs.text:
\(Q=[q]\) types/colors, type fractions \(\alpha=(\alpha_k)\), symmetric \(B=(B_{k\ell})\in\mathbb R_+^{q\times q}\).

\(\text{SBM}_n(B,\alpha)\). Each \(i\in[n]\) independently draws color \(k(i)\sim\alpha\). Conditional on colors, edges are independent with \(\Pr(ij\in E) = B_{k(i),k(j)}/n\).

⇒ A vertex of color \(k\) has \(\text{Bin}(n_\ell, B_{k\ell}/n) \approx \text{Poi}(B_{k\ell}\alpha_\ell) =: \text{Poi}(C_{k\ell})\) neighbors of color \(\ell\). Mean matrix \(C = (C_{k\ell})\).

docs.title: Multitype Branching Process (MTBP)
docs.text:
Type-\(i\) parent has \(\vec X_i = (X_{i1},\dots,X_{iq})\) children, with \(X_{ij}\) = #type-\(j\) children. Joint PGF $\(G_i(\vec\lambda) = \sum_{\vec k}\Pr(\vec X_i=\vec k)\prod_j\lambda_j^{k_j}.\)$ Mean matrix \(C_{ij}=\mathbb E[X_{ij}]\).

SBM example. \(X_{ij}\sim\text{Poi}(C_{ij})\) independent ⇒ \(G_i(\vec\lambda) = \exp\Big(\sum_j C_{ij}(\lambda_j-1)\Big)\).

docs.title: Generation-\(t\) mean
docs.text: Let \(Z_{ij}(t)\) = #type-\(j\) descendants in generation \(t\) from a type-\(i\) root. $\(\mathbb E[Z_{ij}(t+1)\mid \vec Z_i(t)] = \sum_\ell \mathbb E[X_{\ell j}]Z_{i\ell}(t) \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbb E[Z_{ij}(t)] = (C^t)_{ij}.\)$

docs.title: Reducibility / irreducibility
docs.text: \(B\) is reducible iff \(\exists k\neq\ell\) with \((B^t)_{k\ell}=0\) \(\forall t\) (no path between types). Equivalent: types cluster into groups with no inter-group paths. Irreducible = otherwise.

docs.title: Lemma (Subcritical: extinction)
docs.text:
If \(\lambda_{\max}(C) < 1\), then \(\eta_i = \Pr(\text{extinction}\mid\text{root type }i) = 1\) (\(\theta_i = 0\)).

Proof. \(\Pr(\vec Z_i(t)\neq 0) \leq \mathbb E[\sum_j Z_{ij}(t)] = \sum_j(C^t)_{ij} \leq O(t^{q-1}\lambda_{\max}^t) \to 0\). (For symmetric \(C\), drop the \(t^{q-1}\) factor.) So \(\theta_i = \lim_{t\to\infty}\Pr(\vec Z_i(t)\neq 0) = 0\).

docs.title: Lemma 1 (Iterated PGF gives extinction-by-\(t\))
docs.text:
\(\eta_i(t) = \Pr(\vec Z_i(t)=0)\) satisfies \(\eta_i(t+1) = G_i(\vec\eta(t))\), \(\vec\eta(0)=\vec 0\).

Proof (induction). \(t=0\): \(\eta_i(1) = \Pr(\vec X_i=\vec 0) = G_i(\vec 0)\). Step: condition on first generation \(\vec X_i = \vec k\). Each of the \(\sum k_j\) children initiates an indep copy of the BP; all must die in \(t\) generations: $\(\Pr(\vec Z_i(t+1)=0\mid \vec X_i=\vec k) = \prod_j \eta_j(t)^{k_j}.\)$ Sum over \(\vec k\) weighted by \(P_i(\vec k)\) gives \(G_i(\vec\eta(t))\).

docs.title: Lemma 2 (Monotonicity)
docs.text:
\(\vec\eta(t)\uparrow\vec\eta\) where \(\eta_i = \Pr(\text{extinction})\).

(Events \(\{Z_i(t)=0\}\) nested ⇒ probabilities monotone; bounded by 1.)

docs.title: Theorem (Fixed-point characterization)
docs.text:
(a) \(\eta_i(t)\uparrow\eta_i\). (b) \(\vec\eta = \vec G(\vec\eta)\). (c) \(\vec\eta\) is the smallest fixed point in \([0,1]^q\): if \(\vec G(\vec\lambda)=\vec\lambda\) then \(\vec\eta\leq\vec\lambda\).

Proof. (a) Lemmas 1+2. (b) \(G_i\) monotone+continuous in coordinates ⇒ \(\eta_i(t+1)=G_i(\vec\eta(t))\uparrow G_i(\vec\eta)\), but also \(\eta_i(t+1)\uparrow\eta_i\) ⇒ \(\eta_i = G_i(\vec\eta)\). (c) \(\vec G(\vec\lambda)=\vec\lambda\), \(\vec 0\leq\vec\lambda\): \(\eta_i(1)=G_i(\vec 0)\leq G_i(\vec\lambda)=\lambda_i\). Iterate: \(\eta_i(t)\leq\lambda_i\) ⇒ \(\eta_i\leq\lambda_i\).

docs.title: Theorem (Survival ⇔ supercritical)
docs.text:
For irreducible, non-singular MTBP: \(\theta_i = 1-\eta_i > 0\) for all \(i\) ⇔ \(\lambda_{\max}(C) > 1\).

(Singular = each parent has exactly one child a.s., trivially never extinct.)

docs.title: Theorem (Giant component on SBM)
docs.text:
Assume \(C\) irreducible. (a) If \(\lambda_{\max}(C) < 1\): w.h.p. \(|C_{\max}| = O(\log n)\). (b) If \(\lambda_{\max}(C) > 1\): \(\exists\theta>0\) with \(|C_{\max}|/n \to \theta\) in probability, where $\(\theta = \sum_i \alpha_i\theta_i,\qquad \theta_i = 1 - G_i(\vec\eta) = 1 - e^{-\sum_j C_{ij}\theta_j}\;\text{(SBM)}.\)$ Off-giant components: \(\max_{x\notin C_{\max}}|C(x)| = O(\log n)\).

Proof sketch. 1. BFS coupling. Local exploration of SBM at a type-\(i\) vertex couples to MTBP\((C)\) for \(O(\log n)\) generations. 2. Subcritical. All BFS trees stay tiny ⇒ tail bound on \(|C(x)|\) via mean of \(\sum_j(C^t)_{ij} = O(t^{q-1}\lambda_{\max}^t)\). 3. Supercritical. Define \(V_i^+ = \{x: k(x)=i, |C(x)|>K\log n\}\). BFS gives \(|V_i^+|/n \to \theta_i\alpha_i\) in probability. A sprinkling argument (small-tree lemma + connectivity of "big" pieces above a threshold) shows all of \(\bigcup_i V_i^+\) merges into a single giant: \(||C_{\max}|/n - \sum_i\alpha_i\theta_i| \to 0\).

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Use \(\lambda_{\max}(C)\), not the entries of \(C\), as the criticality test.
  • Smallest fixed point of \(\vec G(\vec\eta)=\vec\eta\) is the extinction prob; survival is \(1\) minus that.
  • For SBM with type fractions: \(\theta = \sum_i \alpha_i\theta_i\) (weight by type fraction).

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: Real social networks have two seemingly incompatible features: short typical distances (\(\text{diam} \approx \log n\)) AND high local clustering (\(C_{\text{loc}}\) bounded away from 0). \(G(n,p)\), CM, and PA all satisfy short distances but have \(C_{\text{loc}} \to 0\). Small world models fix this by starting with a network of high local clustering (e.g., a cycle with short-range edges) and adding a few long-range "shortcut" edges that bring the diameter down.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
• Local clustering: \(C_{\text{loc}} = \frac1n \sum_i \frac{\#\text{triangles at }i}{\binom{d_i}{2}}\) — fraction of "two friends of \(i\)" who are also friends.
  • Global clustering: \(C_{\text{gl}} = \frac{\sum_i \#\Delta \ni i}{\sum_i \binom{d_i}{2}}\) — different normalization.
  • Diameter (here): typical pairwise distance in \(C_{\max}\) (abuse of notation, but standard in this course).
  • Small world property: \(\text{diam} = O(\log n)\) AND \(C_{\text{loc}} = \Omega(1)\).

For \(G(n,p), \text{CM}(d), \text{PA}\): \(\text{diam} \asymp \log n\) but \(C_{\text{loc}} \to 0\).

docs.title: 3. Watts-Strogatz model [1998]
docs.text:
1. Start with cycle \(C_n\). Degrees = 2, \(C_{\text{loc}} = 0\), \(\text{diam} = n/2\).
  2. Connect all vertices at distance \(\le k\) on the cycle. Degrees = \(2k\), \(C_{\text{loc}} > 0\), \(\text{diam} = n/(2k)\).
  3. Rewire each edge with prob \(p\): detach one endpoint, reconnect to a uniform random vertex.

Numerical observation. As \(p\) increases from 0, diameter drops fast (driven by long-range edges), while \(C_{\text{loc}}\) stays high (most edges stay local).

Heuristic for diameter (when \(1 \ll kp \ll \text{const}\)): $\(\text{diam} \approx \text{const} \cdot \frac{\log n}{k^2 p}.\)$

Reasoning: - Long-range rewired edges behave like \(G(n, kp/n)\) → \(\text{diam} \approx \log n / \log(kp)\). - Short-range cycle edges further shorten distances by a factor of \(k\).

docs.title: 4. Bollobas-Chung [rigorous result]
docs.text:
• Start with \(C_n\).
  • Add one half-edge to each vertex.
  • Match half-edges uniformly at random.

Theorem. \(\text{diam}(G) / \log_2 n \to_P 1\).

Why \(\log_2\)? Each vertex has 1 long edge + 2 short edges. Locally exploring follows a 2D random walk on (long, short) types with transition matrix \(\Pi = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\), so BFS tree grows at rate \(\nu = \lambda_{\max}(\Pi) = 2\).

docs.title: 5. Newman-Watts [1999] + Barbour-Reiner [2006]
docs.text:
• Local graph as in WS (cycle + short range).
  • Don't rewire — instead add global edges from \(G(n, c/n)\).

Barbour-Reiner Theorem. If \(c \gg 1\) and \(kc \le ext{const}\): $\( rac{ ext{diam}(G)}{\log_ u n} o_P 1, \quad u = \lambda_{\max} egin{pmatrix} c & c \ 2k & 1\end{pmatrix} = frac12 ig[c+1 + \sqrt{(c+1)^2 + 4c(2k-1)} ig].\)$

Limit case (\(kc \ll 1\), \( u \approx 1 + 2k\lambda\)): $\( ext{diam}(G) \approx rac{\log n}{2k\lambda}.\)$

This matches the WS heuristic with \(p \mapsto \lambda\).

docs.title: 6. Kleinberg navigation [2001-02] — the classic result
docs.text:
Setup (Milgram '67). Can a "myopic agent" find a target \(t\) in \(O(\log n)\) steps?

Myopic agent rules: - Can follow short or long edges. - Sees one step ahead. - Knows the physical location of target + possible next moves.

docs.title: Greedy on cycle with one long random edge per vertex
docs.text:
Theorem 1. Greedy needs \(\Theta(\sqrt n)\) steps. Theorem 2. No myopic algorithm does better.

Proof idea (Theorem 1). It takes \(\Theta(\min\{r, n/r\})\) steps to reduce distance from \(r/2\) to \(r\). Set \(r_i = n \cdot 2^{-i}\) for \(i = 1, \ldots, T = \log_2 n\). Total: \(\sum_{i=1}^T \min\{n 2^{-i}, 2^i\} = \Theta(\sqrt n)\).

docs.title: Modified small world model on \(\{0, \ldots, L-1\}^d\) (\(n = L^d\))
docs.text:
• Add local edges for \(\text{dist} \le p\).
  • Each vertex chooses \(q\) long-range edges to \(u_1, \ldots, u_q\) with prob \(\Pr(v \to u_i) \propto 1/d(v, u_i)^\alpha\).

Theorem 3 (Kleinberg). - If \(\alpha = d\) (the "magic" exponent): greedy takes \(\Theta((\log n)^2)\) steps. - If \(\alpha \ne d\): any myopic algorithm takes \(n^c\) steps for some \(c > 0\).

Proof idea. - \(\alpha > d\): too local, \(E[\text{dist}(v,u)] \approx \int r^{d-1} r^{-\alpha} dr\) converges → can't get far enough. - \(\alpha < d\): too random → jumping around blindly, no learning. - \(\alpha = d\): just right — every distance scale is equally likely → can halve distance in \(O(\log n)\) rounds.

docs.title: 7. Household / motif models
docs.text:
• Household models [Trapman '07, Ball '10]: start with \(G\) with \(\text{diam} \approx \log n\), replace nodes by households (cliques or other small graphs).
  • Motif models [Hofstad '15]: start with motifs \(M = (H, d^{\text{ext}})\) — small graphs with external stubs. Match stubs uniformly.

These are how you get both \(\text{diam} = O(\log n)\) AND \(C_{\text{loc}} = \Theta(1)\) without giving up rigor.

docs.title: 8. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Confusing adding edges (Newman-Watts) with rewiring (Watts-Strogatz). Rigor exists for NW; WS is mostly numerical/heuristic.
  • Assuming the WS diameter formula \(\log n / (k^2 p)\) is rigorous — it's a heuristic.
  • For Kleinberg: \(\alpha = d\) (dimension), not \(\alpha = 1\). In 2D grid, the magic exponent is 2.
  • Greedy in 1D Kleinberg model takes \(\Theta(\sqrt n)\), not \(\Theta(\log n)\) — the magic exponent for 1D requires \(\alpha = 1\).

docs.title: 9. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Local clustering + short diameter ⇒ need long-range edges."
  • "Newman-Watts: keep the cycle, sprinkle in \(G(n,c/n)\) on top."
  • "Kleinberg: \(\alpha = d\) is the only exponent that lets greedy navigation be polylog."
  • "Watts-Strogatz heuristic: \(\log n / k^2 p\) — long edges shorten by \(\log n / \log(kp)\), short edges shorten by another factor of \(k\)."

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text: Preferential attachment is a model where a graph grows one vertex at a time, and new edges are added with probability proportional to existing vertex degrees — "rich get richer." This generates power-law degree distributions. The main proof technique is tracking how a single vertex's degree evolves over time using conditional expectations and recurrences.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
Preferential attachment graph \(G_t\): At each time step \(t\), a new vertex arrives and connects to existing vertices with probability proportional to their degrees.

Degree process \(D_i(t)\): The degree of vertex \(i\) at time \(t\). This is a stochastic process.

Conditional expected increment: \(E[D_i(t+1) - D_i(t) \mid G_t]\) — the expected degree gain in one step, given the current graph.

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Write the one-step conditional expectation: \(E[D_i(t+1) \mid G_t] = D_i(t) + f(D_i(t), t)\)
  2. Derive a recurrence for \(E[D_i(t)]\)
  3. Solve the recurrence (often gives power-law growth)
  4. For concentration: use martingale arguments or variance bounds

docs.title: Key recurrence pattern
docs.text:
Setup: At time \(t\), the total degree is \(2t\) (or similar). Vertex \(i\) gains an edge with probability \(D_i(t)/(2t)\).

Conditional expectation: $\(E[D_i(t+1) \mid G_t] = D_i(t) + \frac{D_i(t)}{2t} = D_i(t)\left(1 + \frac{1}{2t}\right)\)$

Taking full expectation and solving: $\(E[D_i(t)] = D_i(i) \cdot \prod_{s=i}^{t-1}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2s}\right) \approx D_i(i) \cdot \sqrt{t/i}\)$

Recognition signal: The \(\sqrt{t/i}\) growth means early vertices (small \(i\)) have much higher expected degree.

docs.title: Concentration for PA degrees
docs.text: Use variance bounds to show degrees concentrate around their expected values.

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting that total degree changes at each step (denominator in attachment probability is time-dependent)
  • Not conditioning on \(G_t\) properly — the increment depends on the current state
  • Confusing the expected degree recurrence with a deterministic recursion
  • In concentration: using bounds that are too loose because you didn't account for the dependence structure

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Rich get richer = degree is proportional to attachment probability"
  • "One-step conditional expectation is the engine of every PA proof"
  • "Early vertices grow like \(\sqrt{t}\), late vertices stay small"
  • "The recurrence IS the proof — write it, solve it, done"

docs.title: Definitions (compact)
docs.text:
• Stochastic matrix \(M\): \(M_{uv} \geq 0\), \(\sum_v M_{uv} = 1\).
  • Homogeneous MC: \(\Pr(X_t = v_t \mid X_0,\dots,X_{t-1}) = M_{v_{t-1} v_t}\).
  • Distribution evolution (row vector): \(\mu_t = \mu_0 M^t\).
  • Stationary: \(\pi M = \pi\).
  • Irreducible: \( orall i,j\;\exists t: (M^t)_{ij}>0\) (digraph \(D_M\) strongly connected).
  • Aperiodic: \(\gcd\{t : (M^t)_{ii}>0\} = 1\) for every SCC.
  • Lazy chain: \(M' = \alpha I + (1-\alpha)M\), \(\alpha\in(0,1)\). Same stationary, always aperiodic.
  • Random walk on \(G\): \(P_{uv} = A_{uv}/d_u\).
  • TV distance: \(\|\mu- u\|_{TV} = \sup_S |\mu(S)- u(S)| = frac12\|\mu- u\|_1\).

docs.title: Lemma 1 (Eigenvalue 1 always exists)
docs.text:
\(\lambda=1\) is an eigenvalue of any stochastic \(M\), and every eigenvalue satisfies \(|\lambda|\leq 1\).

Proof. \(M\mathbf 1 = \mathbf 1\) gives \(\lambda=1\). If \(Mv=\lambda v\), pick \(i\) with \(|v_i|=\max_j|v_j|\): $\(|\lambda||v_i| = \big|\sum_j M_{ij}v_j\big| \leq \sum_j M_{ij}\max_j|v_j| = \max_j|v_j|.\)$

docs.title: Lemma 2 (Existence of stationary distribution)
docs.text:
Any stochastic \(M\) has a stationary distribution \(\pi\in\Delta\).

Proof. Since \(\lambda=1\) is an eigenvalue of \(M^T\), take \(w\neq 0\) with \(wM=w\). Normalize \(\sum |w_i|=1\). Then $\(|w_j|=\big|\sum_i w_i M_{ij}\big| \leq \sum_i |w_i|M_{ij}.\)$ Summing over \(j\): \(\sum_j|w_j| \leq \sum_i|w_i|=1\) — equality, so each inequality is equality, hence \(|w| = |w|M\). Set \(\pi_i=|w_i|\).

docs.title: Lemma 3 (Convergence theorem)
docs.text:
If \(M\) is irreducible and aperiodic, then \(\pi\) is unique with \(\pi(i)>0\) \(\forall i\), and \(\mu_0 M^t \to \pi\) for any \(\mu_0\).

(Standard Perron–Frobenius; proof is HW/text.)

docs.title: Lemma 4 (RW on undirected graph, stationary)
docs.text:
For RW on connected undirected \(G\) with \(P_{uv}=A_{uv}/d_u\): $\(\pi_u = d_u/(2|E|)\quad\text{is stationary.}\)$

Proof. \((\pi P)_v = \sum_u \pi_u P_{uv} = \sum_u \tfrac{d_u}{2|E|}\cdot\tfrac{A_{uv}}{d_u} = \tfrac{1}{2|E|}\sum_u A_{uv} = d_v/(2|E|) = \pi_v\).

docs.title: PageRank
docs.text: PageRank with restart probability \(\alpha\in(0,1)\): $\(\pi = \tfrac{\alpha}{n}\mathbf 1 + (1-\alpha)\pi P, \quad P_{uv}=A_{uv}/d_u^{\text{out}}.\)$ This is \(\pi M = \pi\) for the aperiodic, irreducible chain \(M_{uv} = \alpha/n + (1-\alpha)P_{uv}\).

docs.title: Lemma 5 (PageRank power-series formula)
docs.text:
\[\pi(v) = \frac{\alpha}{n}\sum_{k=0}^\infty (1-\alpha)^k \sum_u (P^k)_{uv}.\]

Proof. Solve \(\pi(I-(1-\alpha)P) = \tfrac{\alpha}{n}\mathbf 1\), expand \((I-(1-\alpha)P)^{-1}=\sum_{k\geq 0}(1-\alpha)^k P^k\).

docs.title: Lemma 6 (Truncation gives geometric error)
docs.text:
\[\Big\|\pi - \tfrac{\alpha}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{K-1}(1-\alpha)^k\mathbf 1 P^k\Big\|_1 \leq (1-\alpha)^K \leq e^{-\alpha K}.\]

Proof. Tail \(= \tfrac{\alpha}{n}\sum_{k\geq K}(1-\alpha)^k \sum_{u,v}(P^k)_{uv} = \tfrac{\alpha}{n}\sum_{k\geq K}(1-\alpha)^k\cdot n = \alpha(1-\alpha)^K/\alpha = (1-\alpha)^K\).

docs.title: Lemma 7 (Mixing of PageRank walk — coupling)
docs.text:
Let \(X_t\) be PR walk from \(x\), \(\tilde X_t\) stationary. Then \(\|\mu_{t,x}-\pi\|_{TV} \leq (1-\alpha)^t \leq e^{-\alpha t}\).

Proof (coupling). Use shared randomness \((Y_t,Z_t)\): \(Y_t\sim\text{Be}(\alpha)\) tells whether to restart, \(Z_t\sim\text{Unif}(V)\) is the restart target. If \(Y_s=1\) for some \(s\leq t\), then \(X_t=\tilde X_t=Z_s\) run forward by the same \(P\)-steps. So $\(\Pr(X_t\neq \tilde X_t) \leq \Pr(\forall s\leq t: Y_s=0) = (1-\alpha)^t.\)$ This bounds \(\|\mu_{t,x}-\pi\|_{TV}\).

docs.title: Common pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting periodicity kills convergence (e.g., bipartite RW). Fix: lazy chain.
  • Forgetting sinks make \(P\) ill-defined (divide by 0); restart fixes it.

docs.title: Mixing Time, Conductance, Spectral Gap
docs.text: Setting: RW on connected undirected \(G=(V,E)\), \(P=D^{-1}A\), stationary \(\pi_i=d_i/(2|E|)\).

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
• \(\mu_{t,x}(v) = (P^t)_{xv}\), \(\bar d(t)=\max_{x,y}\|\mu_{t,x}-\mu_{t,y}\|_{TV}\), \(d(t)=\max_x\|\mu_{t,x}-\pi\|_{TV}\).
  • Mixing time: \(\tau = \min\{t : \bar d(t)\leq 1/e\}\).
  • Symmetrized matrix: \(\tilde P = D^{1/2}PD^{-1/2} = D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\) (symmetric, same eigenvalues as \(P\)).
  • Eigenvalues of \(P\): \(1=\lambda_1\geq \lambda_2\geq\cdots\geq\lambda_n\geq -1\).
  • Spectral gap: \(1-\lambda_2\).
  • Normalized Laplacian: \(\tilde L = I - \tilde P = D^{-1/2}(D-A)D^{-1/2}\), eigenvalues \(\Lambda_i = 1-\lambda_i\in[0,2]\).
  • Flow: \(Q_{ij}=\pi_i P_{ij}=A_{ij}/(2|E|)\).
  • Conductance: \(\phi(S) = \frac{\sum_{i\in S, j\in S^c}Q_{ij}}{\pi(S)\pi(S^c)} = \frac{\text{cut}(S,S^c)\,\text{vol}(V)}{\text{vol}(S)\,\text{vol}(S^c)}\), \(\phi=\min_{\emptyset\neq S\subsetneq V}\phi(S)\).
  • Expansion: \(\alpha(S) = \frac{\text{cut}(S,S^c)}{\min(|S|,|S^c|)}\), \(\alpha=\min_S\alpha(S)\).

docs.title: Lemma 1 (Submultiplicativity of \(\bar d\))
docs.text:
\(\bar d(s+t) \leq \bar d(s)\bar d(t)\), hence \(\bar d(k\tau)\leq e^{-k}\). Also \(d(t)\leq \bar d(t)\leq 2d(t)\).

(Standard coupling argument; HW.)

docs.title: Lemma 2 (Eigenvalues of \(P\) are real, in \([-1,1]\))
docs.text: Proof. \(\tilde P=D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\) symmetric ⇒ real eigenvalues. \(P=D^{-1/2}\tilde P D^{1/2}\) similar to \(\tilde P\) ⇒ same eigenvalues. Bound \(|\lambda|\leq 1\) as in the MC lemma.

docs.title: Lemma 3 (First eigenvector of \(\tilde P\))
docs.text:
\(\varphi_1(v)=\sqrt{\pi(v)}\) satisfies \(\tilde P\varphi_1=\varphi_1\), \(\|\varphi_1\|_2=1\).

Proof. \((\tilde P\varphi_1)_x = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_x}}\sum_y A_{xy}\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_y}}\sqrt{d_y/(2|E|)} = \sqrt{d_x/(2|E|)}=\varphi_1(x)\). Norm: \(\sum\pi(x)=1\).

docs.title: Lemma 4 (Quadratic form of Laplacian)
docs.text:
For \(L=D-A\): \(f^T L f = \tfrac12\sum_{i,j}(f_i-f_j)^2 A_{ij}\).

Proof. RHS \(=\sum_i f_i^2 d_i - \sum_{ij}f_i A_{ij}f_j = f^T(D-A)f\).

docs.title: Lemma 5 (Expansion ↔ Conductance)
docs.text:
\[\frac{\alpha}{d_{\max}} \leq \phi \leq 2\frac{\alpha}{d_{\min}}.\]

Proof. \(\pi(S)=\sum_{x\in S}d_x/(2|E|)\), with bounds \(\frac{d_{\min}|S|}{2|E|}\leq \pi(S)\leq \frac{d_{\max}|S|}{2|E|}\), and \(\min\{\pi(S),\pi(S^c)\} \geq \pi(S)\pi(S^c) \geq \tfrac12\min\{\pi(S),\pi(S^c)\}\). Plug into \(\phi(S)\).

docs.title: Theorem 1 (Cheeger inequality)
docs.text: \[\frac{\phi^2}{8} \leq 1-\lambda_2 \leq \phi.\]

docs.title: Step A — Variational form of \(1-\lambda_2\)
docs.text: $\(1-\lambda_2 = \min_{\varphi\perp\varphi_1}\frac{\varphi^T(I-\tilde P)\varphi}{\varphi^T\varphi}.\)$ This is just Rayleigh on the symmetric \(\tilde L\), with first eigenvector \(\varphi_1\).

docs.title: Step B — Test vector for \(\phi(S)\)
docs.text:
Define \(f_S(i) = \big[\pi(S^c)\mathbf 1_{i\in S} - \pi(S)\mathbf 1_{i\in S^c}\big]\sqrt{d_i}\).

Then (i) \(f_S \perp \varphi_1\), (ii) \(\phi(S) = \dfrac{f_S^T(I-\tilde P)f_S}{f_S^T f_S}\).

Proof of (i). \(\sum_x f_S(x)\varphi_1(x)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2|E|}}\sum_x d_x[\pi(S^c)\mathbf 1_S - \pi(S)\mathbf 1_{S^c}] = \sqrt{2|E|}(\pi(S)\pi(S^c)-\pi(S^c)\pi(S)) = 0\).

Proof of (ii). Let \(\tilde f_i=f_i/\sqrt{d_i}\) so \(\tilde f\) is the two-level \(\pi(S^c)\mathbf 1_S-\pi(S)\mathbf 1_{S^c}\). Then - Numerator: \(f^T(I-\tilde P)f = \tilde f^T(D-A)\tilde f = \tfrac12\sum A_{ij}(\tilde f_i-\tilde f_j)^2\). Only cross-edges contribute, \(|\tilde f_i-\tilde f_j|=\pi(S)+\pi(S^c)=1\) ⇒ \(=\sum_{i\in S, j\in S^c}A_{ij}\). - Denominator: \(f^Tf = \sum_i d_i[\pi(S^c)^2\mathbf 1_S+\pi(S)^2\mathbf 1_{S^c}] = 2|E|[\pi(S)\pi(S^c)^2+\pi(S^c)\pi(S)^2] = 2|E|\pi(S)\pi(S^c)\). - Ratio: \(\frac{\text{cut}(S,S^c)}{2|E|\pi(S)\pi(S^c)} = \phi(S)\).

docs.title: Step C — Upper bound \(1-\lambda_2 \leq \phi\)
docs.text: By (i)+(ii) and Step A: \(1-\lambda_2 \leq \phi(S)\) for every \(S\). Take \(\min\) over \(S\).

docs.title: Step D — Lower bound \(\phi^2/8 \leq 1-\lambda_2\)
docs.text: (Harder; not detailed in lecture but stated.) Construct from second eigenvector a "sweep cut" \(S\) with \(\phi(S)^2 \leq 8(1-\lambda_2)\).

docs.title: Theorem 2 (Mixing time bound from \(\lambda_2\), lazy chain)
docs.text:
For lazy chain \(M=\tfrac12(I+P)\) with eigenvalues \(\tilde\lambda_i=\tfrac12(\lambda_i+1)\in[0,1]\): $\(\tilde\lambda_2^t \leq \bar d(t) \leq \frac{\tilde\lambda_2^t}{\sqrt{\pi_{\min}}},\quad \pi_{\min}=\min_x\pi(x).\)$

Proof of upper bound. Spectral decomp: \(\tilde M=\sum_i\tilde\lambda_i\varphi_i\varphi_i^T\), so \(M^t = D^{-1/2}\tilde M^t D^{1/2}\) gives $\(\mu_{t,x}(v) = M^t_{xv} = \pi(v) + \sum_{i\geq 2}\tilde\lambda_i^t\varphi_i(x)\varphi_i(v)\sqrt{d_v/d_x}\)$ (the \(i=1\) term equals \(\pi(v)\), since \(\varphi_1(x)\varphi_1(v)\sqrt{d_v/d_x}=\sqrt{d_x d_v}/(2|E|)\sqrt{d_v/d_x}=d_v/(2|E|)=\pi(v)\)).

Then $\(\|\mu_{t,x}-\pi\|_1 \leq \tilde\lambda_2^t \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{\min}}}\sum_i|\varphi_i(x)|\sum_v|\varphi_i(v)|\sqrt{d_v}.\)$ By Cauchy-Schwarz with \(\sum_i\varphi_i(x)^2=1\) (since \(\varphi_i\) form an ONB of \(\mathbb R^n\) ⇒ \(\sum_i\varphi_i\varphi_i^T=I\), diagonal entry =1) and \(\sum_i\big(\sum_v\varphi_i(v)\sqrt{d_v}\big)^2 = \sum_v d_v = 2|E|\): $\(\|\mu_{t,x}-\pi\|_1 \leq \tilde\lambda_2^t\sqrt{2|E|/d_{\min}} = \tilde\lambda_2^t/\sqrt{\pi_{\min}}.\)$ Since \(\bar d(t)=\max\tfrac12\|\mu_{t,x}-\mu_{t,y}\|_1\leq\max\|\mu_{t,x}-\pi\|_1\), done.

docs.title: Common pitfalls
docs.text:
• Confusing \(\lambda_n\) (\(-1\) end, periodicity) with \(\lambda_2\) (mixing). The lazy chain pushes everything to \([0,1]\) so only \(\lambda_2\) matters.
  • \(\varphi_1\) is the eigenvector of \(\tilde P\), not of \(P\). The eigenvector of \(P\) for \(\lambda=1\) is \(\mathbf 1\).
  • Test vector \(f_S\) has factor \(\sqrt{d_i}\) (so it lives in the \(\tilde P\) space).

docs.title: Spectral Clustering and Graph Partitioning
docs.text:
Goal: split \(V=S\sqcup S^c\) minimizing some "well-separated" objective. Common objectives, all expressible via \(\phi(S)\): $\(\phi(S) = \frac{\text{cut}(S,S^c)\cdot\text{vol}(V)}{\text{vol}(S)\,\text{vol}(S^c)}, \quad \text{vol}(S)=\sum_{x\in S}d_x.\)$

The eigenproblem: \((D-A)\psi = \Lambda D\psi\) ⇔ \(P\psi = (1-\Lambda)\psi\), with eigenvalues \(0=\Lambda_1\leq\Lambda_2\leq\cdots\leq\Lambda_n\leq 2\).

docs.title: Theorem 1 (Variational characterization of \(\Lambda_2\))
docs.text:
$\(\Lambda_2 = \min_{\psi\,:\,\psi^T D\mathbf 1 = 0}\frac{\psi^T(D-A)\psi}{\psi^T D\psi}.\)$ Any minimizer is a generalized eigenvector \((D-A)\psi=\Lambda_2 D\psi\).

Proof. Substitute \(\varphi = D^{1/2}\psi\). Then \(\psi^T(D-A)\psi = \varphi^T\tilde L\varphi\), \(\psi^T D\psi = \varphi^T\varphi\), and \(\psi^T D\mathbf 1 = 0 \Leftrightarrow \varphi\perp D^{1/2}\mathbf 1 = \sqrt{2|E|}\,\varphi_1\). So this is the standard Rayleigh for \(\tilde L\) restricted to \(\varphi\perp\varphi_1\), giving second eigenvalue \(\Lambda_2\).

docs.title: Theorem 2 (Variational characterization of \(\phi\))
docs.text:
$\(\phi = \min_{\psi\;\text{2-level},\;\psi^T D\mathbf 1=0}\frac{\psi^T(D-A)\psi}{\psi^T D\psi}.\)$ The minimum is attained by \(\psi_S = \pi(S^c)\mathbf 1_S - \pi(S)\mathbf 1_{S^c}\), which gives \(\phi(S)\) exactly.

Proof. From the Mixing-Time note, \(\phi(S) = \frac{f_S^T(I-\tilde P)f_S}{f_S^T f_S}\) for \(f_S = D^{1/2}\psi_S\). Convert via \(\psi=D^{-1/2}\varphi\): \(\phi(S)=\frac{\psi_S^T(D-A)\psi_S}{\psi_S^T D\psi_S}\).

Conversely, any 2-level \(\psi=a\mathbf 1_S+b\mathbf 1_{S^c}\) with \(\psi^T D\mathbf 1=0\) forces \(a\,\text{vol}(S)+b\,\text{vol}(S^c)=0\) ⇔ \(\psi\propto\psi_S\). So the constrained min equals \(\min_S\phi(S)=\phi\).

docs.title: Cheeger upper bound (consequence)
docs.text: \(\Lambda_2 \leq \phi\) since the 2-level constraint is more restrictive.

docs.title: Spectral algorithm (sweep cut)
docs.text:
1. Compute \(\psi_2\) with \((D-A)\psi_2 = \Lambda_2 D\psi_2\).
  2. Order vertices \(\psi_2(v_1)\leq\psi_2(v_2)\leq\cdots\leq\psi_2(v_n)\).
  3. Output \(S_k=\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}\) with \(k\in\{\) sign-cut, median-cut, \(\arg\min_k\phi(S_k)\) (sweep) \(\}\).

Certificate. \(\Lambda_2\leq\phi\leq\phi(S_{\text{out}})\), so error \(\phi(S_{\text{out}})-\phi \leq \phi(S_{\text{out}})-\Lambda_2\).

docs.title: Lemma (Connected-components characterization)
docs.text:
\(\Lambda_1=\cdots=\Lambda_k=0\) ⇔ \(G\) has \(k\) connected components.

Proof. \((D-A)\psi=0\) ⇔ \(\psi^T(D-A)\psi=\tfrac12\sum A_{ij}(\psi_i-\psi_j)^2=0\) ⇔ \(\psi\) constant on each connected component. Eigenspace dimension = #components.

docs.title: Two-block planted model (worked example)
docs.text:
Adjacency: within-block weight \(a\) or \(b\), between-block weight \(\varepsilon\). Block sizes \(n_1, n_2\). Degrees \(D_1=an_1+\varepsilon n_2\), \(D_2=bn_2+\varepsilon n_1\).

Solve \(A\psi=\lambda D\psi\), conjecturing \(\psi\) constant on each block: \(\psi_u=c_1\) on \(V_1\), \(c_2\) on \(V_2\). The \(2\times 2\) reduced eigenproblem: $\(\begin{pmatrix} an_1 & \varepsilon n_2\\ \varepsilon n_1 & bn_2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}c_1\\c_2\end{pmatrix} = \lambda\begin{pmatrix}D_1 c_1\\ D_2 c_2\end{pmatrix}.\)$ Solutions: \(\lambda=1\) (with \(c_1=c_2\), the trivial one) and \(\tilde\lambda = \frac{n_1 n_2(ab-\varepsilon^2)}{D_1 D_2}\).

Lemma (Recovery condition). The non-trivial eigenvector is \(\lambda_2\) and the sign-cut returns the true blocks ⇔ \(ab>\varepsilon^2\).

(Intuition: as \(\varepsilon\to 0\) the cut goes to 0 and \(\lambda_2\to 1\), \(\Lambda_2\to 0\); spectral recovers exactly.)

docs.title: \(k\)-way clustering
docs.text: Embed \(i\mapsto v_i = (\psi_1(i),\dots,\psi_d(i))\in\mathbb R^d\) (top-\(d\) generalized eigenvectors). Then run \(k\)-means: $\(S=\arg\min\sum_{\ell=1}^k\sum_{i\in S_\ell}\|v_i-m_\ell\|^2,\quad m_\ell=\tfrac{1}{|S_\ell|}\sum_{i\in S_\ell}v_i.\)$

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• The eigenproblem is generalized: \((D-A)\psi=\Lambda D\psi\), not \((D-A)\psi=\Lambda\psi\).
  • The orthogonality is \(\psi^T D\mathbf 1=0\), not \(\psi^T\mathbf 1=0\).
  • \(\Lambda\) ordering is reversed vs \(\lambda\) of \(P\): \(\Lambda_i = 1-\lambda_i\), so \(\Lambda_2\) corresponds to \(\lambda_2\) (second largest of \(P\)).

docs.title: Models
docs.text:
Linear Threshold (LT). Weights \(b_{uv}\geq 0\), thresholds \(\theta_v\in[0,1]\) uniform i.i.d. at \(t=0\). Active set evolves $\(A_t = A_{t-1}\cup\Big\{v\notin A_{t-1}:\sum_{w\in A_{t-1}: wv\in E} b_{wv}\geq\theta_v\Big\}.\)$

Independent Cascade (IC). Probabilities \(p_{uv}\). When \(u\) first becomes active, it gets one chance to activate each inactive out-neighbor \(v\) with probability \(p_{uv}\) (independent).

Influence: \(I(A) = \mathbb E[\lim_t |A_t| \mid A_0=A]\).

Influence-Max problem: Find \(A^*\) with \(|A^*|=k\) maximizing \(I(A)\). NP-hard (reduction from Vertex Cover, KKT'03).

docs.title: Submodularity
docs.text: Def. \(f:2^V\to\mathbb R^+\) is submodular if for all \(v\in V\), \(S\subseteq T\subseteq V\): $\(f(S\cup\{v\})-f(S) \;\geq\; f(T\cup\{v\})-f(T).\)$ ("Diminishing returns".) Monotone: \(f(S\cup\{v\})\geq f(S)\).

docs.title: Lemma 1 (Convex combinations preserve submodularity)
docs.text:
If each \(f_i\) is monotone+submodular and \(p_i\geq 0\), then \(f=\sum_i p_i f_i\) is too.

Proof. \(f(S\cup v)-f(S)=\sum_i p_i[f_i(S\cup v)-f_i(S)] \geq \sum_i p_i[f_i(T\cup v)-f_i(T)] = f(T\cup v)-f(T)\).

docs.title: Lemma 2 (IC influence is submodular)
docs.text:
Random digraph reduction. Pre-flip all coins \(X_{uv}\sim\text{Be}(p_{uv})\); let \(E(X)\) = open edges. The cascade from seeds \(A\) activates exactly \(C(A) = \bigcup_{v\in A}C(v)\) where \(C(v)\) = vertices reachable from \(v\) in \(D(X)=(V,E(X))\). Then \(I(A) = \mathbb E_X[|C_X(A)|]\).

Submodularity of \(|C_X(\cdot)|\) (each fixed \(X\)). Fix \(S\subseteq T\). The "new" reachable set when adding \(v\) is \(C_X(v)\setminus C_X(S)\). Since \(C_X(S)\subseteq C_X(T)\): $\(|C_X(S\cup v)| - |C_X(S)| = |C_X(v)\setminus C_X(S)| \geq |C_X(v)\setminus C_X(T)| = |C_X(T\cup v)|-|C_X(T)|.\)$

Take expectation over \(X\) — Lemma 1 finishes.

docs.title: Theorem (Greedy is \((1-1/e)\)-approximation)
docs.text:
For monotone submodular \(f\) with \(f(\emptyset)=0\), the greedy algorithm - Start \(A_0=\emptyset\). - \(A_t = A_{t-1}\cup\{v^*\}\) where \(v^*=\arg\max_{v\notin A_{t-1}} f(A_{t-1}\cup\{v\})\).

produces \(A_k\) with $\(f(A_k) \geq \big(1-(1-1/k)^k\big)\max_{|A|=k}f(A) \geq (1-1/e)\,\text{OPT}.\)$

docs.title: Step 1 (Key claim).
docs.text:
For \(|S|\leq k\) and \(S^*\) optimal of size \(k\): $\(\max_{x\notin S}\big[f(S\cup\{x\})-f(S)\big] \geq \tfrac{1}{k}\big[f(S^*)-f(S)\big].\)$

Proof. Let \(S^*\setminus S = \{v_1,\dots,v_p\}\), \(p\leq k\). Telescoping: $\(f(S^*) \leq f(S\cup S^*) = f(S) + \sum_{i=1}^p\big[f(S\cup\{v_1,\dots,v_i\}) - f(S\cup\{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\})\big].\)$ Submodularity bounds each term by \(f(S\cup\{v_i\})-f(S)\): $\(f(S^*) - f(S) \leq \sum_i [f(S\cup v_i)-f(S)] \leq k\max_{x\notin S}[f(S\cup x)-f(S)].\)$

docs.title: Step 2 (Greedy unrolling).
docs.text: With \(S=A_{t-1}\), greedy picks the maximizer: $\(f(A_t) \geq f(A_{t-1}) + \tfrac{1}{k}[f(A^*)-f(A_{t-1})] = \tfrac{1}{k}f(A^*) + (1-\tfrac{1}{k})f(A_{t-1}).\)$ Iterate from \(A_0=\emptyset\): $\(f(A_k) \geq \tfrac{1}{k}f(A^*)\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}(1-\tfrac{1}{k})^i = f(A^*)\big(1-(1-\tfrac{1}{k})^k\big) \geq (1-1/e)f(A^*).\)$ (Used \(\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}(1-1/k)^i = k(1-(1-1/k)^k)\) and \((1-1/k)^k\leq e^{-1}\).)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Submodularity proof: must condition on \(X\) (the random digraph) first; otherwise \(C(\cdot)\) is random.
  • Greedy gives \(1-1/e\approx 0.632\), not exact optimum (NP-hard).
  • LT is also submodular (KKT'03), but proof is harder (uses a different randomization — not covered).

docs.title: 1. What this topic is about
docs.text:
The stochastic SIR model describes how an infection spreads through a population. Each infected individual infects susceptible ones at rate \(\beta = c/n\) and recovers at rate \(\gamma = 1\). The key insight is that the early stage of an epidemic looks like a branching process, and the survival probability of that branching process determines whether a large outbreak occurs.

The main result: for \(c > 1\), a large outbreak happens with probability \(P = 1 - 1/c\). For \(c \leq 1\), the epidemic always dies out.

docs.title: 2. Core objects / definitions
docs.text:
SIR dynamics: Infected individual \(i\) stays infected for \(T_i \sim \text{exp}(1)\). During that time, infects susceptible \(j\) if infection clock \(T_{ij} \sim \text{exp}(\beta)\) fires before recovery, i.e., \(T_{ij} < T_i\).

Infection probability given recovery time: $\(p_T = \Pr(T_{ij} < T) = 1 - e^{-\beta T}\)$

Offspring distribution: Conditioned on \(T\), number infected is \(X \mid T \sim \text{Bin}(n, p_T)\).

Basic reproduction number: \(R_0 = E[X] = \frac{n\beta}{1+\beta} = \frac{c}{1+c/n} \to c\) as \(n \to \infty\).

Generating function (limiting): $\(G_c(\lambda) = \frac{1}{1 + c(1-\lambda)}\)$

Extinction probability: Smallest solution of \(\eta = G_c(\eta)\), which gives \(\eta = 1/c\) for \(c > 1\).

docs.title: 3. Standard proof moves
docs.text:
1. Condition on recovery time \(T\): Write offspring as \(\text{Bin}(n, p_T)\) conditional on \(T \sim \text{exp}(1)\)
  2. Compute generating function: Integrate over \(T\) to get \(G_n(\lambda)\)
  3. Take \(n \to \infty\) limit: Use dominated convergence to get \(G_c(\lambda)\)
  4. Solve fixed-point equation: \(G_c(\eta) = \eta\) gives extinction probability
  5. For tail bounds below criticality: Use moment generating function + Chernoff-style bounds

docs.title: Lemma: Infection probability
docs.text:
Statement: \(p_T = 1 - e^{-\beta T}\) where \(T' \sim \text{exp}(\beta)\).

Why it matters: This converts the continuous-time infection process into a probability you can plug into a binomial.

Proof: \(p_T = \Pr(T' < T) = \int_0^T \beta e^{-\beta t'} dt' = 1 - e^{-\beta T}\).

docs.title: Lemma: Limiting generating function
docs.text:
Statement: \(G_n(\lambda) \to G_c(\lambda) = \frac{1}{1+c(1-\lambda)}\) for \(\lambda \in [0,1]\).

Why it matters: Links the finite-population SIR model to a simple branching process.

Proof idea: 1. Write \(G_n(\lambda) = \int_0^\infty (1-(1-\lambda)p_T)^n e^{-T} dT\) 2. Show \((1-(1-\lambda)p_T)^n \to e^{-cT(1-\lambda)}\) pointwise (since \(np_T \to cT\)) 3. Apply dominated convergence (integrand \(\leq 1\), \(e^{-T}\) integrable) 4. Evaluate: \(\int_0^\infty e^{-T(1+c(1-\lambda))} dT = \frac{1}{1+c(1-\lambda)}\)

docs.title: Lemma: Survival probability
docs.text:
Statement: For \(\beta = c/n\): survival probability \(\to 0\) if \(c \leq 1\), and \(\to 1 - 1/c\) if \(c > 1\).

Proof idea: Solve \(G_c(\eta) = \eta\): $\(\eta(1 + c(1-\eta)) = 1 \iff (1-\eta)(1-c\eta) = 0\)$ Solutions: \(\eta = 1\) and \(\eta = 1/c\). Smallest is \(1/c\) (for \(c > 1\)), so \(P = 1 - 1/c\).

docs.title: Lemma: Concentration below criticality
docs.text:
Statement: For \(c < 1\): \(\Pr(|T_{\text{SIR}}^{n,\beta}| > k) \leq e^{-\frac{(1-c)^2}{4}k}\).

Why it matters: Outbreak size has exponential tail when below threshold.

Proof idea: \(|T| > k\) implies \(X_1 + \cdots + X_k \geq k\). Apply Chernoff bound with rate function \(I(x) \geq \frac{(x-c)^2}{2x(x+1)}\).

docs.title: Lemma: Rate function bound
docs.text:
Statement: For \(c \leq x \leq 1\): $\(I(x) \geq x\log\frac{x}{c} + (1+x)\log\frac{1+c}{1+x} \geq \frac{(x-c)^2}{2x(1+x)}\)$

Proof idea: 1. Bound MGF: \(E[e^{tX}] \leq \frac{1}{1-c(e^t-1)}\) (using \(p_T \leq \beta T\) and \(1+x \leq e^x\)) 2. Optimize Chernoff bound: \(I(x) \geq \sup_t \{tx + \log(1-c(e^t-1))\}\) 3. Optimal \(t_0 = \log\frac{(1+c)x}{c(1+x)}\) 4. Taylor expand around \(x=c\) to get quadratic lower bound

docs.title: 5. Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting to condition on recovery time \(T\) before computing offspring distribution
  • Using \(np_T = c\) instead of \(np_T \to cT\) (the \(T\) matters!)
  • Not checking dominated convergence conditions (need integrand bounded by integrable function)
  • Confusing extinction probability \(\eta\) with survival probability \(P = 1 - \eta\)
  • In the fixed-point equation: \(\eta = 1/c\) is the extinction prob, not \(c\) itself
  • In concentration proofs: the rate function bound requires \(c \leq x \leq 1\), not just \(x \geq c\)

docs.title: 6. Quick memory hooks
docs.text:
• "Condition on \(T\), then it's just a binomial" — this unlocks everything
  • "\(R_0 = c\) is the threshold, survival prob = \(1 - 1/c\)"
  • "Generating function \(G_c(\lambda) = 1/(1+c(1-\lambda))\) — memorize this"
  • "Below threshold: outbreak dies exponentially fast, rate \((1-c)^2/4\)"
  • "DCT is the magic word for \(G_n \to G_c\)"

docs.title: SIR on Networks
docs.text: Companion to SIR and Epidemics.md (homogeneous mixing); this note covers SIR on \(G(n,p)\) and the configuration model, plus the SIR ODEs.

docs.title: Setup: Infection digraph
docs.text: Each vertex draws recovery time \(T_i\sim\mu\) i.i.d. Conditional on \(\{T_i\}\), for each edge \(ij\) independently draw it as "open" with prob \(p_{T_i} = 1-e^{-\beta T_i}\). \(C_+(v)=R(\infty)=\) vertices ever-infected starting from \(v\) = those reachable in the infection digraph.

docs.title: Theorem 1 (Bow-tie / two-stage BP for \(G(n,p)\) SIR, \(p=c/n\))
docs.text:
For homogeneous mixing on \(K_{n+1}\) or \(G(n,p)\): - If \(R_0 = c\mathbb E[p_T] < 1\): \(R_\infty\) is bounded in probability. - If \(R_0 > 1\): \(\Pr(R_\infty\geq n^{1/3}) \to P>0\), and conditioned on \(R_\infty\geq n^{1/3}\), \(R_\infty/n\to\theta>0\) where $\(\theta + e^{-\theta R_0} = 1,\quad\text{equivalently}\quad 1-P = \mathbb E_T[e^{-cP\,p_T}].\)$

Proof sketch (BFS + concentration). Track \(N_t\) = #susceptibles after \(t\) infections, \(\Delta_t\) = #ever-infected. Each newly infected node draws \(X_t\sim\text{Bin}(N_{t-1}, \tfrac{c}{n}p_{T_t})\) new infections. By independence the BP approximation \(X_t^+\sim\text{Bin}(n, \tfrac{c}{n}p_T)\) couples to \(X_t\) with error \(O(t/n)\), valid for \(t\leq n^{1/3}\). Subcriticality (rate function bound, see the SIR and Epidemics note) gives \(\Pr(R_\infty\geq k)\leq e^{-(1-c)^2 k/4}\). Above criticality, BFS leaves the BP regime and a deterministic LLN takes over: \(\Delta_t/n\to f(t/n)\) on the rescaled time, fixed point \(\theta+e^{-c\theta}=1\) (general \(\mu\): replace \(c\) by \(R_0\)).

docs.title: Rigorous proof of the supercritical fluid limit
docs.text:
Lemma 7.2. There exists \(\alpha>0\) such that for \(n\) large, \(R_0>1\), and \(0<\epsilon\leq 1/2\), with prob \(\geq 1 - O(e^{-\alpha\epsilon^2 t})\): $\(f_{-\epsilon}(t/n) \leq \tilde Y_t/n \leq f_{+\epsilon}(t/n) + O(1/n),\quad f_{\pm\epsilon}(x) = (1\pm\epsilon)\big(1-e^{-(1\pm\epsilon)R_0 x}\big) - x.\)$

Setup. \(\tilde Y_0=1\), \(\tilde Y_{t+1} = \tilde Y_t + X_{t+1} - 1\), with \(X_t\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}, T_t \sim \text{Bin}(N_{t-1}, p_t)\) where \(p_t = (c/n) p_{T_t}\) and \(p_{T_t} = 1 - e^{-\beta T_t}\). Then \(|C_+(x)| = \min\{t : \tilde Y_t \leq 0\}\).

Why this is harder than the plain giant-component argument. Two layers of randomness: the durations \(T_i\) (which determine \(p_i\)), and the binomial neighbor draws given \(T_i\). We apply multiplicative Chernov twice — once on \(\sum p_i\) over the \(T_i\)'s, once on \(\Delta_t\) given the \(T_i\)'s.

Proof.

(i) Conditional binomial structure. By the same survival-coupling as in P2, conditional on \(T_1,\ldots,T_t\): $\(\Delta_t = X_1+\cdots+X_t \sim \text{Bin}\Big(n-1,\;1-\prod_{i=1}^t(1-p_i)\Big),\qquad \tilde Y_t = \Delta_t + 1 - t.\)$

(ii) Sandwich \(\prod(1-p_i)\) and concentrate \(\sum p_i\). For \(n\geq 2c\) we have \(p_i\leq c/n\leq 1/2\), so Taylor of \(\log(1-p_i)\) gives $\(-p_i(1+c/n) \leq \log(1-p_i) \leq -p_i,\)$ and exponentiating: \(e^{-(1+c/n)\sum p_i} \leq \prod_{i=1}^t(1-p_i) \leq e^{-\sum p_i}\).

Now \(\sum p_i = (c/n)\sum_i Y_i\) where \(Y_i = 1 - e^{-\beta T_i}\in[0,1]\) are i.i.d. with mean \(\bar p\). The multiplicative Chernov bound on \(\sum Y_i\) gives, with prob \(\geq 1 - 2e^{-\epsilon^2 \bar p t/4}\): $\((1-\epsilon)R_0\,\tfrac{t}{n} \leq \sum_{i=1}^t p_i \leq (1+\epsilon)R_0\,\tfrac{t}{n}\quad(R_0 := c\bar p).\)$ On this event: $\(e^{-(1+c/n)(1+\epsilon)R_0\,t/n} \leq \prod_{i=1}^t(1-p_i) \leq e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}.\tag{7.6}\)$

(iii) Concentrate \(\Delta_t\) given the \(T_i\)'s. On (7.6), use \(1-e^{-x}\geq x(1-e^{-1})\) for \(x\in[0,1]\): $\(\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t] \geq (n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big) \geq \tfrac{1-\epsilon}{2}t \geq t/4\quad(\epsilon\leq 1/2,\;n \text{ large}).\)$ Multiplicative Chernov (P1) applied to \(\Delta_t \mid T_1,\ldots,T_t\) then gives, with prob \(\geq 1 - 2e^{-\epsilon^2 t/16}\): $\((1-\epsilon)\,\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t] \leq \Delta_t \leq (1+\epsilon)\,\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t].\tag{7.7}\)$ Both events (7.6) and (7.7) hold with prob \(\geq 1 - O(e^{-\alpha\epsilon^2 t})\), \(\alpha = \min(\bar p/4, 1/16)\).

(iv) Lower envelope. Combining (7.6) and (7.7): $\(\Delta_t \geq (1-\epsilon)(n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big),\)$ so $\(\tilde Y_t \geq 1 - t + (1-\epsilon)(n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1-\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big) \geq n\,f_{-\epsilon}(t/n).\)$

(v) Upper envelope. On the upper side of (7.6): $\(\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid T_1,\ldots,T_t] \leq (n-1)\big(1 - e^{-(1+\epsilon)(1+c/n)R_0\,t/n}\big) \leq n\big(1-e^{-(1+\epsilon)R_0\,t/n}\big) + O(t/n),\)$ where the second step uses \(e^{-(1+c/n)R_0 t/n} = e^{-R_0 t/n}\cdot e^{-O(t/n^2)}\) and \(t \leq n\). Combined with (7.7): $\(\tilde Y_t \leq 1 - t + (1+\epsilon)\mathbb E[\Delta_t\mid\cdot] \leq n(1+\epsilon)\big(1-e^{-(1+\epsilon)R_0 t/n}\big) - t + O(1) = n\big(f_{+\epsilon}(t/n) + O(1/n)\big).\)$

This establishes Lemma 7.2. \(\square\)

Corollary 7.3 (final outbreak size). If \(R_0>1\) and \(k_n\to\infty\) with \(k_n/n\to 0\), then conditioned on \(R_\infty\geq k_n\), \(R_\infty/n\to\theta\) in probability, where \(\theta+e^{-R_0\theta}=1\). Proof: repeat the giant-component argument (iv)–(viii) with \(c\) replaced by \(R_0\) — geometry of \(f_{\pm\epsilon}\) is structurally identical.

Method extraction (two-layer Chernov). - Recognition. A process where each step has both a "type/weight" attached (here \(T_i\)) and an inner random outcome (here \(\text{Bin}(N_{t-1},p_i)\)). - First move. Condition on the types, get binomial inner structure. Then concentrate the sum-of-types separately as \([0,1]\)-valued sum via P1. - Pitfall. If you don't peel the layers, you'll end up with \(\sum(1-e^{-\beta T_i})\) inside an exponential and lose the Chernov hook. Also: \(p_i \leq c/n\) is required for the Taylor sandwich on \(\log(1-p_i)\) to drop higher-order terms. - Threshold takeaway. \(R_0 = c\bar p\) is the natural epidemic threshold — it's the offspring mean of the BFS-equivalent branching process averaged over recovery times.

docs.title: Lemma (Forward/backward BP perspective)
docs.text:
For the bow-tie picture: let \(\eta = \Pr(\text{forward BP dies})\), \(\eta^* = \Pr(\text{backward BP dies})\). Then $\(1-P = G_+(1-P) = \mathbb E_T[e^{-cp_T P}],\qquad 1-\theta = G_-(1-\theta) = e^{-R_0\theta}.\)$ Here \(G_+, G_-\) are the offspring PGFs of forward/backward BPs, with \(G_-\) using degree-biased distribution.

(Same fixed-point shape as homogeneous mixing; the network just changes the offspring law.)

docs.title: SIR ODE limit (homogeneous mixing, \(G(n,c/n)\))
docs.text:
Let \(S(t)\) = #susceptibles, \(X(t)\) = #unexplored edges out of infectives, \(I(t)\) = #infected.

Stochastic dynamics (Markov rates): - At rate \(\beta X\): explore one edge. With prob \(S/n\), the other endpoint is susceptible → it becomes infected, gains \(\text{Poi}(c)\) new edges. Either way \(X\to X-1\). - At rate \(\gamma\) each, an infective recovers (\(d_v\to 0\)).

docs.title: Theorem 2 (LLN for SIR ODE)
docs.text:
As \(n\to\infty\), \((S/n, X/n, I/n)\to(s,x,i)\) deterministic, with $\(\dot s = -\beta x s,\qquad \dot x = -(\beta+\gamma)x - c\dot s,\qquad \dot i = -\gamma i - \dot s.\)$

(Proof = differential-equation method à la Darling-Norris; quoted.)

docs.title: Lemma (Final size formula)
docs.text:
\(s(\infty) = s(0)\,e^{-R_0(s(0)-s(\infty))}\) where \(R_0 = c\beta/(\beta+\gamma) = c\bar p\) (\(\bar p = \mathbb E[p_T]\)).

Proof. From \(\dot x = -(\beta+\gamma)x - c\dot s\) integrate \(0\to\infty\) with \(x(0)=x(\infty)=0\): $\(0 = -(\beta+\gamma)\int_0^\infty x\,dt - c(s(\infty)-s(0))\;\Rightarrow\; \beta\int x = \tfrac{c\beta}{\beta+\gamma}(s(0)-s(\infty)).\)$ And \(\dot s/s = -\beta x\) gives \(s(\infty) = s(0)e^{-\beta\int x}\). Combine.

docs.title: SIR on Configuration Model CM(\(d\))
docs.text:
Track: - \(X(t)\) = #active half-edges (incident to infectives, unmatched) - \(S_d(t)\) = #susceptible vertices of degree \(d\) - \(U(t)\) = #unmatched half-edges total

Rate-of-change identities (when a \(\beta\)-clock rings, pair an active half-edge with a uniform unmatched one): $\(\dot U = -2\beta X,\qquad \dot S_d = -\beta d\,X/U,\qquad \dot X = -(\beta+\gamma)X - \beta X^2/U + \beta\sum_d d(d-1)S_d/U.\)$

docs.title: Lemma (Closed-form via \(\theta\)-reduction)
docs.text:
Define \(\theta(t) = e^{-\beta\int_0^t X/U}\). Then $\(U = U(0)\theta^2 = n\bar d\,\theta^2,\qquad S_d = S_d(0)\theta^d = p_d n\,\theta^d.\)$

Proof. \(\dot U/U = -2\beta X/U\) ⇒ integrate. \(\dot S_d/S_d = -\beta d\,X/U\) ⇒ integrate.

docs.title: Theorem 3 (Single ODE for \(\theta\))
docs.text:
$\(\frac{1}{\beta+\gamma}\dot\theta = -F(\theta),\qquad F(\theta) = \bar p\big(1 - G^*(\theta)\big) - (1-\theta),\)$ where \(G^*(\theta) = \sum_k \tfrac{(k+1)p_{k+1}}{\bar d}\theta^k\) is the PGF of the size-biased excess degree, and \(\bar p = \beta/(\beta+\gamma)\).

Proof sketch. Use \(\beta\sum_d d(d-1)S_d/U = \beta n\sum d(d-1)p_d\theta^{d-2}/(n\bar d) = \beta\,\partial_\theta G^*(\theta)\), plug into \(\dot X/X\), then with \(\psi:=\theta X/U\) derive a linear ODE in \(\psi\) with solution \(\psi=\tfrac{\beta+\gamma}{\beta}(\theta-1)-(G^*(\theta)-1)\). Combine with \(\dot\theta = -\beta\psi\).

docs.title: Lemma (Final outbreak size)
docs.text:
Let \(\theta_\infty\) = smallest root of \(F(\theta)=0\) in \((0,1)\). Then $\(R_\infty/n \;\to\; 1 - G(\theta_\infty),\quad G(\theta)=\sum_k p_k\theta^k.\)$

Why this matches forward/backward BP. \(1-\theta_\infty = \bar p(1-G^*(\theta_\infty))\) is exactly the backward-BP fixed point (\(\eta^* = G^*(1-\bar p(1-\eta^*))\) in disguise), and \(1-G(\theta_\infty)\) is the forward survival \(1-G(\eta^*\)-style pullback\()\).

docs.title: Force of infection / "infection runs out of high-degree first"
docs.text: $\(\frac{1}{S_k}\dot S_k = -\frac{k}{\theta}(\beta+\gamma)F(\theta).\)$ Force is proportional to \(k\) ⇒ high-degree susceptibles deplete fastest.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• For CM, the excess degree \(G^*\) enters offspring (forward BP), not the raw \(G\).
  • \(R_0 = c\mathbb E[p_T] = c\bar p\); missing the \(\bar p\) factor (averaging over recovery time) is the most common slip.
  • \(\theta\) is not an extinction probability — it is the survival of an outgoing half-edge.

docs.title: 📖 hello-algo — Reading Notes
docs.text:
🏠 Home · PDF: hello-algo_1.3.0_en_python.pdf (in vault root)

Workflow

Read the chapter that matches this week's LeetCode topic. Don't just read — re-type the Python implementation from memory, then check. That's active recall.

docs.title: Ch1 Encounter Algorithms
docs.text:
1. Input -> Algorithms -> Output

  2. Data Structure: A process full of trade-offs

  3. Data structures are the foundation of algorithms. Data structures provide algorithms with structured storage of data and methods for operating on data.

  4. Data structures store data information, algorithms solve specific questions
  5. Algorithms can be implemented based on different data structures, but execution efficiency may vary greatly. choosing the appropriate data structure is key

docs.title: Ch2 Complexity Analysis
docs.text:
1. Algorithm design:
    • Find a solution to the problem
    • Seek optimal solution: time efficiency and space efficiency
  2. repeatedly executing a task is very common.

docs.title: Iteration: solving bottom up
docs.text:
1. for loop: suitable for use when the number of iterations is known in advance we usually use "for i in range(a, b) because it is a iteration variable time complexity is a linear relationship with n
    • for loop in python automatically handle i += 1
  2. while loop: you need to initialize condition variable i by yourself, the a in range, change i by yourself while has better flexible because we can adjust the condition variable by ourself like twice per round.
  3. nested loop: nest one loop inside another quadratic relationship, n^2

docs.title: Recursion: solving top-down
docs.text:
• Having a function call itself. Consists of two phases
    1. Descend: The program continuously calls itself deeper, usually passing in smaller or more simplified parameters, until reaching a "termination condition"
    2. Ascend: When trigger the condition, the program returns layer by layer from the deepest recursive function, aggregating the result of each layer
  • Implementation perspective. Consist of three elements:
    1. Termination condition
    2. Recursive call: corresponds to descending, where function calls itself with smaller parameters.
    3. Return result, corresponds to ascending
  • Call stack: function's context data is stored in a memory area called "stack frame space"; consumes more memory space than iteration
    • function calls incur additional overhead: to call a function, the computer will do the following work hidden, thus is also less time-efficient than loops
      • Set up the call stack — save where you came from
      • Pass arguments — copy the values into the function
      • Allocate memory — create space for local variables
      • Return — clean up and go back to where you came from
    • Before termination triggered, there are n unreturned recursive exist simultaneously, with a recursion depth of n.
  • Tail Recursion: if a function makes the recursive call as the very last step before returning, the function can be optimized by the compiler to have space efficiency but not automatically support by compiler (eg: python), so still risk stack overflow issue
  • Recursion Tree: Used a lot when using "divide and conquer" subproblem decomposition
    • One call produces two call branches
  • "Last-in, first-out" of stack

docs.title: Time Complexity
docs.text: It counts the growth trend of the algorithms's runtime as the data volume increases - Asymptotic upper bound of function: how to find upper bound f(n)? 1. Count the number of operation - Ignore constants in T(n) - Omit all coefficient - Use multiplication for nested loops - Ranking - Constant O(1) - Linear O(n) - Quadratic O(n^2) - Exponential O(2^n) - Logarithmic O(logn): Reducing to half each round. To be precise, dividing into m corresponds to a O(log_m n) = O(log_k n/ log_k m) = O(log_k n) so m can be omitted

docs.title: Worst, Best, and Average Time complexities
docs.text: • Use random data distribution to estimate average time complexity

docs.title: Space Complexity
docs.text:
• Input space
  • Temporary space
    • Temporary data
    • stack frame space
    • instruction space
  • Output space
  • Only focus on worst-case space complexity
    • Based on the worst input data
    • based on the peak memory during algorithm execution
  • Ranking:
    • O(1)
    • O(n): arrays, linked lists, stacks, queue, recursion
    • O(n^2): matrices and graphs
    • O(2^n): Binary trees
    • O(logn)

docs.title: Ch3 Data Structure
docs.text:
• Arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, hash tables, trees, heaps, and graphs.

    • Logical structure: data is arranged in a certain order
      • Linear: one-to-one sequential relationship
      • Non-linear
        • Tree structures: elements have a one-to-many relationship
    • Physical structure: reflects the way data is stored in computer memory Memory resources are important consideration, if there is a lack of contiguous large memory blocks, the data structure must be able to be stored in dispersed memory spaces
      • Contiguous space storage (arrays) Memory space for linked list nodes are one for value one for pointer All data structures are implemented based on arrays, linked lists, or a combination of both. Stack and queues can be implemented using either ways, hash tables may include both.
    • Linked list: can change the length, "dynamic data structure"
    • Array: cannot change length, "static data structure"

  • Basic data type:

    • CPU can directly operate on
      • Integer type: byte, short, int, long
      • Floating-point types: float, double, used to represent decimal numbers
      • Character: char
      • Boolean: bool
    • Binary form in computer: one binary bit is 1 bit. 1 byte consists of 8 bits. Each programming language has its own data type definitions

docs.title: Array
docs.text:
• without initial values: start with 0s
  • with given initial values
  • Assessing elements is efficient, O(1)
  • Inserting elements/ Removing element: very inefficient, need to shift all elements related and will have loss of elements or memory waste
  • traverse arrays: can traverse by index or directly iterating through each element. enumerate() a function that automatically helps you loop through an iterate, keeping track of the index of the item for you
  • Advantages:
    • High space efficiency
    • O(1) for random access any element
    • Used for sorting and searching algorithms like quick sort, merge sor, binary search....; lookup tables

docs.title: Linked List
docs.text: Node + reference allows nodes to be stored scattered throughout the memory - Head node - Tail node: points to null, nullptr, 'None' in Java, C++, Python - O(1) for add and remove - O(n) for accessing a node - Common types: - Singly linked list - Circular linked list: Round-robin scheduling algorith - Doubly linked list: has references for both directions, requires more memory space - Used for stacks and queues; hash tables; graphs - You can use for fruit in fruits

docs.title: Building ChatGPT from Scratch
docs.text: An umbrella project made of many small, self-contained builds — each one implementing a single stage of the ML pipeline by hand. The point isn't a competitive model; it's to walk the whole path from raw text to a served model myself, so that later, when I reach for a high-level library, I know exactly what it's doing for me.

docs.title: The rule I'm holding to
docs.text: Build each piece once, by hand, with nothing high-level doing the interesting part — then swap in the production tool knowing what it replaced. Every sub-project below is small enough to finish and test on its own, and each one leans on a reference note elsewhere on this site for the theory behind it.

docs.title: The pipeline, as a series of small projects
1 · Data & representation
docs.text:
• BPE tokenizer from scratch — turn raw text into tokens and back, and see why it splits words the way it does (un + related).
  • Data pipeline — a corpus into train/validation splits, then batched (input, target) windows the model can actually train on.

docs.title: 2 · A first language model
docs.text:
• Bigram baseline — the smallest possible language model. Its real job is to stand up the training loop, the loss, and sampling, so everything after this is a swap of the model, not the scaffolding.
  • Embeddings + a small MLP — token and positional embeddings feeding a tiny network, trained on top of the hand-written autograd I built earlier (manual backprop, no framework). → Neural Networks

docs.title: 3 · Attention & the transformer
docs.text:
• Self-attention by hand — compute Q/K/V, scale, softmax, apply the causal mask; single head first, then multi-head. This is the mechanism the whole thing turns on. → Transformers & Attention
  • The transformer block — attention + feed-forward + residual connections + layer norm, stacked into a small GPT (minGPT scale). → Language Models

docs.title: 4 · Training
docs.text: • The training loop for real — AdamW, a learning-rate schedule, gradient clipping, checkpointing, mixed precision: the difference between "loss goes down" and "loss goes down reliably." → Optimization

docs.title: 5 · Evaluation
docs.text: • Measuring it — loss curves, perplexity, and qualitative sampling on held-out text, so "is it better?" has an answer instead of a vibe.

docs.title: 6 · Alignment
docs.text: • Supervised fine-tuning — adapt the base model to follow instructions, then a conceptual look at preference tuning (RLHF / DPO): how a model goes from "predicts text" to "does what you asked." → Self-Supervised Learning

docs.title: 7 · Inference & serving
docs.text:
• Fast, controllable generation — a KV cache, plus the sampling knobs (temperature, top-k, top-p) that decide how the output actually reads.
  • Serve it — wrap the model behind a small API, reusing the reliability ideas from the other project: timeouts, idempotency, bounded blast radius. → Postmortem Memory Agent

docs.title: Why build it this way
docs.text: This is the whole thesis of the repo in one project: powerful tools amplify you once you have a foundation of your own. By the time an import hides attention or the training loop, I want to have written both at least once — so the library is a shortcut I understand, not a black box I depend on.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Early and building out. The pieces I've already done by hand — an autograd engine, and reading minGPT line by line to find where Q/K/V, multi-head, and the position embeddings live — feed straight into this. Each stage above gets its own small build and lands here as it's finished.

docs.title: Postmortem Memory Agent
docs.text: A project I'm hardening from a 12-hour hackathon prototype into something that could actually run in production — using Designing Data-Intensive Applications (DDIA) as the engineering lens.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
The agent ingests incident postmortems, builds a searchable memory of past failures, and uses that memory to review pull requests and generate follow-up artifacts — flagging changes that look like they could repeat a mistake the team has already made and paid for.

The prototype worked as a demo. The interesting part — and the reason it pairs so well with DDIA — is everything that stands between "works in a demo" and "won't quietly corrupt its own memory at 3 a.m."

docs.title: The core idea
docs.text:
One principle ties the whole redesign together:

Store the facts as an append-only log of incidents (the source of truth). Everything else — the vector memory, the generated artifacts, the review decisions — is derived data that can be rebuilt from that log. Every action that touches the outside world must be idempotent, replayable, and bounded in blast radius.

Get that right and the agent stops being a fragile demo and becomes a small data system.

docs.title: Two kinds of failure
docs.text:
A reviewer agent can fail in two directions, and they don't cost the same:

  • False-block — it flags a safe change. Annoying; slows people down.
  • False-safe — it lets a dangerous change through. This is the expensive one: the incident it was supposed to prevent happens again.

So the whole thing is tuned to spend false-blocks to buy down false-safes — and that trade-off is written down rather than left implicit.

docs.title: What DDIA changes, theme by theme
docs.text: DDIA theme What it fixes in the agent Reliability — fault ≠ failure A single bad model response shouldn't crash an ingest. Wrap the fragile boundaries so a fault degrades instead of taking the system down. Storage & the vector index Understand what the vector store is actually doing — approximate nearest-neighbour recall can miss, and a miss here is a false-safe. Encoding & evolution The memory schema will change. Old records have to keep loading, so every field is versioned and read defensively. Transactions & the dual-write problem Writing to two stores can diverge. Pick one authority and rebuild the other from it; make record IDs content-addressed so re-ingesting the same postmortem is a no-op. Unreliable systems Every external call gets a timeout, bounded retries, and a blast-radius limit, so one hung dependency can't stall everything. Batch & stream processing Reframe the memory as a derived view of the incident log, with a rebuild path that replays the log — which is also how schema upgrades and embedding swaps happen safely. Derived data & architecture Write the reliability targets and the architecture down: source of truth, derived views, blast radius, and the rebuild story.

docs.title: Where it's going
docs.text: The end state isn't "more features" — it's engineering stability: a system whose failure modes I can name, whose memory I can rebuild from scratch, and whose architecture I can explain end to end. The same backbone — an append-only log with everything else derived and replayable — is the foundation I want under the other agent-tooling ideas I'm circling.

Signal What "good" looks like Communication I said the plan out loud before typing Clarifying I asked about constraints and edge cases first Brute → optimal I named the naive version and why I moved off it Complexity I stated time and space before coding Correctness Clean run, edge cases handled Follow-ups I could extend it when the interviewer pushed

docs.text: Date Problem Pattern Difficulty Result 2026-07-16 Two Sum Hashmap Easy ✅ 2026-07-16 Remove Element Two pointers Easy ✅

docs.title: Patterns cheat-sheet
docs.text:
• Two pointers — sorted array, pair sums, in-place dedup / overwrite.
  • Sliding window — longest / shortest substring under a constraint.
  • Hashmap — seen-before, frequency counts, complement lookup.
  • BFS — shortest path / level order · DFS — all paths, connectivity.
  • DP — "count ways / min cost / can we?" with overlapping subproblems.
  • Backtracking — permutations, combinations, subsets, board placement.

docs.title: Pattern drills
docs.text:
When should a hashmap be your first instinct?

When the problem asks "have I seen this before?", needs frequency counts, or wants a complement / pair lookup in \(O(1)\). Two Sum is the archetype.

What signals a sliding window?

A contiguous subarray or substring, plus a constraint to keep satisfied ("longest with ≤ k distinct", "smallest sum ≥ target"). Grow the right edge, shrink the left when the constraint breaks.

BFS vs DFS — how do I pick?

Shortest path / fewest steps / level-by-level → BFS with a queue. Enumerate all paths, test connectivity, or recurse over structure → DFS with a stack or recursion.
