docs.title: Remove Element
docs.text: 🔗 LeetCode · Easy · Arrays / Two Pointers

docs.title: Prompt (in my own words)
docs.text: Given an array nums and a value val, remove every occurrence of val in place and return the new length k. The first k slots of nums must hold the kept elements (any order); whatever sits past k doesn't matter.

docs.title: Clarifying questions
docs.text:
• Does the order of the kept elements matter? (No — that's what makes the overwrite trick legal.)
  • Do I shrink the array, or just report k? (Just k; the grader reads the first k slots.)
  • Could every element be val, or none of them? (Yes to both — k may be 0 or len(nums).)
  • Extra memory allowed? (Aim for \(O(1)\) — that's the point of "in place".)
Try it before you scroll

Keep a write pointer. As a read pointer sweeps the array, what's the rule for when the write pointer advances?

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
Brute force — O(n) time, O(n) space

Build a new list of everything that isn't val, then copy it back. Works, but it allocates a second array — it ignores the "in place" ask.

Optimal — two pointers, O(1) space

A slow write pointer k and a fast read pointer i. Sweep with i; each time nums[i] != val, copy it to nums[k] and advance k. Elements equal to val are skipped, so they get overwritten. At the end, k is the new length.

The one thing to remember

"Remove in place, order doesn't matter" = a write pointer that only advances on keepers. The read pointer never waits.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: Approach Time Space Copy to new list \(O(n)\) \(O(n)\) Two pointers \(O(n)\) \(O(1)\)

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def removeElement(self, nums: List[int], val: int) -> int:
        k = 0                       # write pointer: next slot for a keeper
        for i in range(len(nums)):  # read pointer
            if nums[i] != val:
                nums[k] = nums[i]
                k += 1
        return k
Tests — run to check 
def check(nums, val):
    k = Solution().removeElement(nums, val)
    print(f"k={k}, kept={sorted(nums[:k])}")

check([3, 2, 2, 3], 3)               # k=2, kept=[2, 2]
check([0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 0, 4, 2], 2)   # k=5
check([2, 2, 2], 2)                  # k=0

docs.title: Dry run
docs.text:
nums = [3, 2, 2, 3], val = 3:

  • i=0 → 3 == val → skip, k=0
  • i=1 → 2 != val → nums[0]=2, k=1
  • i=2 → 2 != val → nums[1]=2, k=2
  • i=3 → 3 == val → skip
  • return k=2; the first two slots are [2, 2] ✓

docs.title: Follow-ups
docs.text:
What if you also had to minimise writes?

Swap the element at i with the one at the end and shrink the range, instead of copying forward. Fewer writes when val is rare, since you only touch elements you actually move.

How is this the same as 'Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array'?

Same write-pointer skeleton — only the keep condition changes (there it's nums[i] != nums[k-1]).

docs.title: Retro
docs.text:
What tripped me up

Don't mutate the list's length while scanning. The write pointer overwrites in place; you never delete, you just report k.

Flashcard — Q: remove-in-place when order is free? → A: a write pointer that advances only on keepers. (→ DSA deck)

docs.title: Two Sum
docs.text: 🔗 LeetCode · Easy · Arrays / Hashmap

docs.title: Prompt (in my own words)
docs.text: Given an array of integers and a target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to the target. Assume exactly one solution, and you can't use the same index twice.

docs.title: Clarifying questions
docs.text:
• Is the array sorted? (No — so a two-pointer scan would need a sort first, which loses the original indices.)
  • Exactly one valid pair, or could there be none or several?
  • Can I reuse the same element? (No.)
  • Any negatives or duplicates? (Yes to both — the approach has to survive them.)
Try it before you scroll

You get one pass. What would let you answer "have I already seen the number that completes this pair?" in \(O(1)\)?

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
Brute force — O(n²)

Check every pair (i, j) with two nested loops. Correct, but quadratic.

Optimal — O(n) with a hashmap

Walk the array once, keeping a map of value → index for everything seen so far. For each n, the number that completes the pair is target - n. If that complement is already in the map, you've found the answer; otherwise record n and move on.

The one thing to remember

A hashmap turns "have I seen the complement?" into an \(O(1)\) lookup. That's the core move behind almost every pair-sum problem.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: Approach Time Space Brute force \(O(n^2)\) \(O(1)\) Hashmap \(O(n)\) \(O(n)\)

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
from typing import List

def two_sum(nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]:
    seen = {}  # value -> index
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        if target - n in seen:
            return [seen[target - n], i]
        seen[n] = i
    return []
Tests — run to check 
cases = [
    (([2, 7, 11, 15], 9), [0, 1]),
    (([3, 2, 4], 6),       [1, 2]),
    (([3, 3], 6),          [0, 1]),
]
for args, expected in cases:
    got = two_sum(*args)
    print(f"{'✅' if got == expected else '❌'} two_sum{args} = {got}  (expected {expected})")

docs.title: Dry run
docs.text:
nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9:

  • i=0, n=2 → need 7, not in map → seen = {2: 0}
  • i=1, n=7 → need 2, it's in the map → return [0, 1] ✓

docs.title: Follow-ups
docs.text:
What if the array were already sorted?

Two pointers from both ends: sum too big → move the right pointer in, too small → move the left pointer out. \(O(1)\) space. Catch: you'd lose the original indices, so you'd sort (value, index) pairs first.

What about 3-sum?

Fix one element and run this two-sum on the rest → \(O(n^2)\) overall. Sorting first makes it easy to skip duplicates.

docs.title: Retro
docs.text:
What tripped me up

Recording seen[n] = i has to come after the complement check. Do it before, and an element can pair with itself.

Flashcard — Q: pair-sum in one pass? → A: hashmap value → index, check the complement before inserting. (→ DSA deck)
